cards.subject: Business Associations
cards.front: When an Agent Binds the Principal
cards.back:
Core rule
A principal becomes contractually bound when the agent acts through actual authority, apparent authority, or a transaction the principal later ratifies. The allocation of liability also changes depending on whether the principal was disclosed, unidentified, or undisclosed and on what the third party knew about the agency.

Elements / test
- Establish agency: the principal and agent assented that the agent would act for the principal while remaining subject to the principal's control.
- Identify a binding source: express or implied actual authority, apparent authority, or later ratification by the principal.
- Confirm that the transaction fell within the authority being relied upon.
- Apply principal-status rules: with a disclosed principal, the principal normally bears contract liability; with an unidentified principal, both principal and agent are liable; with an undisclosed principal, the agent is liable and the principal also becomes liable when discovered.

Exceptions / limitations
- Conduct outside the agent's authority ordinarily does not bind the principal unless the principal ratifies it.
- A third party who knows the agent lacks authority cannot hold the principal on that supposed authority.
- An undisclosed principal is not bound when the agent was authorized to contract only in the agent's own name.
- A novation can replace the agent with the principal as the contracting obligor.
- Agency by estoppel may bind a principal whose conduct reasonably caused the third party to believe an agency relationship existed.

Memory line
Authority first; principal type second.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Business Associations
cards.back:
Core rule
Apparent authority comes from the principal's manifestations to a third party. If those manifestations reasonably lead the third party to believe the agent may act, the principal can be bound even though the agent had no actual authority.

Elements / test
- Look for words, conduct, prior dealings, or a position supplied by the principal rather than a claim made only by the agent.
- The third party's understanding that the agent was authorized must be objectively reasonable.
- That understanding must be traceable to something the principal communicated or allowed, including holding the agent out or placing the agent in a customary role.
- The third party must have acted in reliance on the apparent authority when entering the transaction.

Exceptions / limitations
- Apparent authority fails when the third party's belief is unreasonable or the third party has direct notice of the limitation.
- An agent cannot create apparent authority merely by announcing that the agent has it.
- Once an affected third party receives notice that authority was withdrawn, apparent authority ends as to that party.
- An undisclosed principal cannot be bound through apparent authority because the principal made no manifestation to the third party.
- Authority inferred from a job title reaches only acts normally performed by someone in that position.

Memory line
Principal to third party = apparent authority.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Business Associations
cards.back:
Core rule
Actual authority is based on communications from the principal to the agent. It may be stated directly or implied from what is reasonably needed or customary to carry out the principal's instructions, and it can bind the principal even if the third party never heard those instructions.

Elements / test
- Find a manifestation from the principal to the agent, not a representation to the third party.
- Ask what a reasonable agent would understand the principal wanted the agent to do.
- Express authority is permission given in spoken or written words.
- Implied authority covers acts reasonably necessary to accomplish the assigned objective and acts normally associated with the agent's position.
- The agent must stay within the reasonable limits of the authority granted.

Exceptions / limitations
- Actual authority can end through revocation, renunciation, agreement, expiration, completion, death or incapacity, or a change making performance illegal.
- After actual authority ends, the agent cannot bind the principal on that basis.
- Apparent authority may continue for third parties who have not received notice of the termination.
- The scope depends on an objectively reasonable reading of the principal's manifestation, not the agent's private belief.
- Some transactions require written authorization, including acts covered by the equal-dignities rule.

Memory line
Principal to agent = actual authority.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Business Associations
cards.back:
Core rule
A general partnership arises when at least two people carry on a profit-seeking business as co-owners. It can be created by conduct without a filing or written agreement, and the parties need not intend to use the word partnership. Sharing net profits supports a partnership inference, and partners then owe fiduciary duties and face joint-and-several responsibility for partnership debts.

Elements / test
- There must be two or more participants.
- They must associate to operate an ongoing business rather than merely share a single transaction under the usual rule.
- Co-ownership is shown especially through shared control and shared profits.
- The enterprise must be conducted for profit.
- No special filing or written instrument is necessary; the relationship may arise from behavior.
- Receipt of net profits ordinarily creates a rebuttable inference of partnership.

Exceptions / limitations
- Profit-based payments do not trigger the inference when they represent debt repayment, wages, rent, an annuity, loan interest, or payment for goodwill.
- A person who holds herself out, or permits herself to be held out, as a partner may be liable to a third party who extends credit in reliance on that representation.
- Co-owning property by itself does not establish a partnership.
- Sharing gross revenue, rather than net profits, does not create the normal partnership inference.
- A minority of jurisdictions also demand an intent to associate specifically as co-owners.

Memory line
Conduct, control, and profits can create the partnership.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Business Associations
cards.back:
Core rule
A partnership is bound by a partner's contract when the partner acts in the ordinary course of the partnership's business or possesses actual authority. General partners are jointly and severally responsible for partnership obligations, while a properly formed LLP generally protects partners from personal contract liability. Under RUPA's exhaustion approach, partnership assets are normally pursued before an individual partner's property.

Elements / test
- Begin with a validly existing partnership.
- The contracting partner must have actual authority or apparent authority arising from an act in the ordinary business of the partnership.
- The agreement must have been made for the partnership rather than solely for the partner personally.
- Classify the entity: general partners have joint-and-several exposure; a limited partnership's general partners have full liability while limited partners are usually capped at their contributions; LLP partners are generally shielded from entity debts.
- For collection, a creditor ordinarily must first look to partnership assets before reaching an individual partner.

Exceptions / limitations
- A newly admitted partner is not personally answerable for obligations incurred before admission, although the partner's contribution may be at risk.
- A dissociated partner remains responsible for obligations arising before dissociation and may face limited post-dissociation exposure for up to two years under the notice rules.
- The liability shield applies only if LLP status was properly created and maintained.
- A partner remains personally liable for that partner's own wrongful conduct.
- An unauthorized agreement outside the partnership's ordinary business does not bind the partnership.

Memory line
Ordinary business usually binds the partnership.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Business Associations
cards.back:
Core rule
Dissolution ends the partnership's ordinary operations and starts the winding-up phase. During winding up, the entity continues only to settle its affairs. Dissolution can follow withdrawal from an at-will partnership, expiration or consent in a term partnership, a court order, illegality, or another statutory event; the partnership terminates only after winding up is finished.

Elements / test
- Identify a trigger: notice of withdrawal in an at-will partnership; expiration or unanimous consent in a term partnership; judicial dissolution for deadlock, misconduct, or impracticability; or illegality by operation of law.
- After dissolution, the partnership continues solely for winding-up activities.
- Winding up includes collecting or selling assets, satisfying obligations, and distributing any remaining value.
- Apply distribution order: nonpartner creditors, partner-creditors, return of capital, and then remaining profits.
- The legal entity ends after the winding-up process is complete.

Exceptions / limitations
- Before winding up is completed, the partners may sometimes agree to continue the business if the governing statute permits it.
- A partner who wrongfully causes dissolution may owe damages and may lose the right to participate in management during winding up.
- Filing a statement of dissolution can give constructive notice and cut off lingering apparent authority after 90 days.
- Transactions reasonably connected to winding up can still bind the partnership after dissolution.
- A partner may ask a court to supervise the winding-up process.

Memory line
Dissolution starts the closing process; termination ends it.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Business Associations
cards.front: Director Duty of Care and Business Judgment
cards.back:
Core rule
Directors must use the care, diligence, and skill an ordinarily prudent director would use in similar circumstances, while acting honestly for the corporation's benefit. The business judgment rule presumes a disinterested, informed, good-faith decision with a rational business basis was proper. A claimant ordinarily must show grossly deficient process, disloyal self-interest, or bad faith to overcome that protection.

Elements / test
- Measure the director's conduct against an ordinarily prudent person serving in a similar position under comparable circumstances.
- The presumption applies when the director acted in good faith, obtained the important information reasonably available, had a rational basis, and lacked a personal financial stake.
- When the presumption applies, a court will not reweigh the wisdom of the business decision itself.
- If the plaintiff rebuts the presumption, the director generally bears the burden of proving entire fairness.

Exceptions / limitations
- The business judgment rule does not protect conflicted or self-dealing transactions; those fall under loyalty principles.
- Gross negligence in the decision-making process can defeat the presumption, while ordinary negligence usually cannot.
- A director may honestly rely on reports or advice from officers, employees, experts, and board committees.
- A charter may eliminate monetary damages for an ordinary care breach, but not for disloyalty, bad faith, or intentional wrongdoing.
- The duty demands a sound process, not a successful result; honest business mistakes remain protected.

Memory line
Courts protect honest process, not guaranteed success.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Business Associations
cards.front: Director Conflicts and Duty of Loyalty
cards.back:
Core rule
When a director has a meaningful financial interest in a corporate transaction, the deal is subject to the duty of loyalty and can be avoided unless it is properly cleansed. Validation may come from informed approval by disinterested directors, informed approval by disinterested shareholders, or proof that the transaction was fair to the corporation when made.

Elements / test
- The director, or a close family member or affiliate, must have a material financial interest in a transaction involving the corporation.
- Absent a safe harbor, the corporation may avoid the transaction.
- Entire fairness combines fair process or fair dealing with a substantively fair price.
- The interested director ordinarily carries the burden of establishing fairness.

Exceptions / limitations
- A conflict alone does not automatically nullify the deal; a valid safe harbor can cleanse it.
- Approval by disinterested decision-makers is effective only after disclosure of the conflict and all important facts.
- Some jurisdictions exclude interested directors when determining the quorum for approval.
- Fully informed shareholder ratification after the transaction may cure the loyalty problem.
- Until validated or affirmed, the deal remains avoidable at the corporation's election rather than automatically void.

Memory line
Disclose, approve, or prove fairness.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Business Associations
cards.back:
Core rule
Every partner owes loyalty to both the partnership and the other partners. A partner may not appropriate partnership property or opportunities, compete with the partnership during its life, or deal adversely with it without authorization. The agreement may reasonably tailor this duty, but ordinarily may not erase it altogether.

Elements / test
- The obligation runs from each partner to the entity and to fellow partners.
- The duty bars personal appropriation of partnership assets or opportunities, competition during the partnership, and undisclosed adverse or self-interested dealings.
- Available remedies include profit disgorgement, compensatory damages, a constructive trust, an accounting, and injunctive relief.
- A particular conflicted transaction may be approved through full disclosure and consent of all partners.

Exceptions / limitations
- The partnership agreement may identify or modify loyalty obligations only if the change is not manifestly unreasonable; total elimination is generally forbidden.
- Dissociation ends the forward-looking loyalty duty, but not responsibility for matters arising while the person was a partner.
- Former partners may compete after dissolution, subject to duties connected with winding up.
- The contractual obligation of good faith and fair dealing operates alongside the statutory loyalty duty.
- All partners may ratify an otherwise disloyal transaction if they act with full knowledge of the facts.

Memory line
No secret profits, competition, or adverse dealing.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Business Associations
cards.back:
Core rule
A derivative lawsuit allows a shareholder to pursue a corporate claim that the corporation has not enforced. The right and any recovery belong to the corporation, unlike a direct action based on injury to the shareholder personally. The plaintiff must satisfy ownership, demand, representation, and other procedural requirements.

Elements / test
- The plaintiff must have owned shares when the challenged conduct occurred and must continue owning them throughout the case.
- The shareholder generally must first demand that the board sue, or specifically plead a recognized basis for demand futility, such as director interest, lack of independence, or inability to exercise honest judgment.
- The plaintiff must fairly and adequately represent the interests of similarly situated shareholders.
- Any judgment or settlement recovery ordinarily goes to the corporation, not directly to the shareholder bringing suit.
- A special litigation committee may evaluate whether pursuing the case serves the corporation; courts review its conclusion under standards that range from business-judgment deference to enhanced scrutiny.

Exceptions / limitations
- Claims involving individual shareholder rights, including voting, declared dividends, or inspection, are direct and do not use derivative procedures.
- Demand rules vary: Delaware recognizes Aronson and Rales futility analyses, while the MBCA generally requires demand even when futility is alleged.
- The corporation must be joined as a nominal defendant because the claim belongs to it.
- A derivative settlement cannot become effective without court approval.
- The same transaction can create both direct and derivative injuries, and a court may separate those claims.

Memory line
Ask whose right was harmed and who receives the recovery.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Business Associations
cards.back:
Core rule
A promoter who signs for a corporation that has not yet been formed is normally personally liable on the agreement. The later corporation can adopt the contract, but adoption alone does not discharge the promoter; release requires a novation or clear agreement that only the future entity would be liable. Separately, a court may disregard limited liability when ownership and the entity are effectively indistinguishable and honoring the entity form would produce fraud or injustice.

Elements / test
- Promoter-contract analysis:
- The promoter purported to act for an entity that did not yet legally exist.
- The starting rule places personal contract liability on the promoter.
- The formed corporation is not bound automatically; it must adopt the agreement expressly, such as by board action, or implicitly, such as by knowingly accepting its benefits.
- Corporate adoption does not release the promoter unless all relevant parties agree to a novation. Veil-piercing analysis:
- Look for alter-ego facts such as commingled funds, inadequate capitalization, ignored formalities, or total domination by one owner.
- Also require an inequitable consequence, fraud, injustice, or defeat of public policy if the separate entity is respected.
- Jurisdictions differ: some demand both unity and injustice, while others balance a broader set of factors.

Exceptions / limitations
- The promoter avoids personal liability when the contract clearly limits recourse to the future corporation.
- A novation requires assent, express or implied, from the promoter, the corporation, and the third party.
- Courts treat veil piercing as an exceptional remedy rather than the ordinary result of poor corporate practice.
- Passive or minority owners are less likely to face personal liability without facts showing their own misuse of the entity.
- Piercing occurs more readily for involuntary tort creditors than for contract creditors who chose to deal with the entity.
- Courts usually apply a similar alter-ego analysis to LLCs, although the required showing can vary by jurisdiction.

Memory line
Adoption binds the corporation; novation frees the promoter.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Civil Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
A federal court may hear a diversity case when the amount in controversy is greater than $75,000, excluding interest and costs, and complete diversity existed when the complaint was filed. Complete diversity means that no plaintiff shares state citizenship with any defendant, and an individual's citizenship ordinarily follows domicile.

Elements / test
- The pleaded amount must exceed $75,000; exactly $75,000 is insufficient.
- Every plaintiff must be diverse from every defendant.
- Citizenship is measured on the filing date.
- An individual is a citizen of the state of domicile, which requires presence plus an intent to remain indefinitely.
- A corporation is a citizen of each state of incorporation and of the one state containing its principal place of business or nerve center.
- A partnership or LLC takes the citizenship of every partner or member.
- Alienage jurisdiction can cover a dispute between a state citizen and a foreign citizen, but ordinarily not a dispute containing only foreign citizens on opposing sides.

Exceptions / limitations
- Federal diversity jurisdiction does not extend to the granting of divorce, alimony, or child-custody decrees.
- The probate exception excludes probating a will or administering an estate.
- One plaintiff may add together separate claims against one defendant, but different plaintiffs ordinarily may not combine their amounts.
- In an ordinary Rule 23 class action, the named representatives control citizenship and at least one qualifying class member must satisfy the amount rule under the applicable doctrine.
- CAFA expands jurisdiction for many class actions involving at least 100 members, more than $5 million in aggregate controversy, and minimal diversity.
- A collusive assignment or similar device used solely to manufacture diversity will not support jurisdiction.

Memory line
Complete diversity plus more than $75,000.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Civil Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Federal-question jurisdiction covers civil claims arising under federal constitutional, statutory, or treaty law. The federal issue normally must appear in the plaintiff's properly pleaded claim, not in a defense. A state-law claim can qualify only when it necessarily raises a disputed, substantial federal issue whose federal adjudication will not upset the federal-state division of judicial work. No monetary threshold applies.

Elements / test
- The federal issue must be visible from the face of the plaintiff's well-pleaded complaint.
- Federal law must supply an essential component of the plaintiff's cause of action.
- A predicted federal defense or a federal counterclaim does not satisfy the well-pleaded-complaint rule.
- There is no minimum amount in controversy for jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. § 1331.
- For an embedded federal issue under Grable, the issue must be necessarily raised, genuinely disputed, important to the federal system, and resolvable federally without disrupting the congressionally intended balance.

Exceptions / limitations
- A defense based on federal law cannot create original federal-question jurisdiction even if it will dominate the litigation.
- A counterclaim, including a compulsory one, cannot provide the federal question needed for the plaintiff's action.
- For a declaratory-judgment suit, ask whether the corresponding coercive action would itself arise under federal law.
- Complete preemption can recharacterize a nominal state claim as federal when Congress made the federal remedy exclusive.
- Article III alone is not enough; Congress must also have supplied statutory jurisdiction, ordinarily through § 1331.

Memory line
The federal issue must appear in the plaintiff's claim.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Civil Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Diversity citizenship is fixed when the lawsuit begins. A person is a citizen of the state where the person is domiciled; a corporation belongs to each incorporation state and its single nerve-center state; and an unincorporated entity inherits every citizenship held by its members or partners.

Elements / test
- Use the parties' citizenship on the filing date rather than the date of the underlying events.
- For an individual, domicile combines physical presence with an intent to stay for an indefinite period.
- A corporation has citizenship in every incorporation state and at its principal place of business.
- Under Hertz, the principal place of business is the location from which senior officers direct, control, and coordinate the company.
- Partnerships, LLCs, and other unincorporated associations are citizens of every state represented among their owners or members.
- A representative for a decedent, minor, or incompetent is assigned the citizenship of the represented person.
- In a standard class action, the citizenship of the named class representatives is used.

Exceptions / limitations
- A later move or other post-filing change in citizenship ordinarily neither creates nor destroys diversity.
- Foreign persons generally carry the citizenship of their nation; treatment of permanent residents has varied under statutory rules and should be applied carefully.
- Assignments or arrangements collusively designed to produce diversity are disregarded.
- A specialized federal statute may define corporate citizenship more broadly for its own jurisdictional scheme.

Memory line
People have domicile; corporations have incorporation plus nerve center.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Civil Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Personal jurisdiction is a court's authority to bind a defendant. The forum must have a statutory basis, usually its long-arm law, and the exercise must satisfy due process. Due process ordinarily requires constitutionally adequate contacts plus a result consistent with fair play and substantial justice.

Elements / test
- Confirm that the forum's jurisdiction statute reaches the defendant.
- Then test constitutional minimum contacts and overall fairness.
- General jurisdiction covers a defendant who is at home in the forum, normally an individual's domicile or a corporation's incorporation state or principal place of business; specific jurisdiction requires a suit arising from or related to forum contacts.
- Traditional grounds also include personal service while voluntarily present, domicile, consent, and waiver.

Exceptions / limitations
- Voluntary physical presence and in-state service can support transient jurisdiction, but presence obtained through coercion is treated differently.
- A defendant can consent or waive the objection through a general appearance, an enforceable forum clause, or failure to assert the defense on time.
- Under Rule 12, the objection must be included in the first Rule 12 motion or, if no such motion is made, in the first responsive pleading.
- A federal court usually borrows the forum state's personal-jurisdiction reach, although a federal statute may authorize nationwide service.
- Internet activity may be assessed through purposeful-interactivity principles associated with Zippo or through Calder's intentional-effects analysis.

Memory line
Statute first, due process second.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Civil Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Specific jurisdiction exists when the defendant deliberately established forum contacts and the particular claim arises from or sufficiently relates to those contacts, with jurisdiction remaining reasonable. Ford Motor allows relatedness without strict but-for causation, but still requires a meaningful connection between the forum-directed conduct and the lawsuit.

Elements / test
- The defendant, through its own choices, must purposefully avail itself of or direct conduct toward the forum.
- The cause of action must arise from or relate closely to those forum contacts.
- Fairness factors include the defendant's burden, the forum's regulatory interest, the plaintiff's access to relief, efficiency, and shared interstate policies.
- Placing a product into commerce without additional forum targeting is ordinarily insufficient; look for purposeful direction under the competing stream-of-commerce formulations.
- Under Calder, an intentional act aimed at the forum and expected to cause harm there can support jurisdiction.

Exceptions / limitations
- Contacts that are merely random, accidental, or attenuated do not establish purposeful availment.
- The unilateral conduct of the plaintiff or another person cannot supply the defendant's required forum connection.
- A contract with a forum resident is not enough by itself, although negotiations, continuing obligations, terms, and performance can collectively establish purposeful contact.
- Even adequate contacts can rarely be outweighed by such severe unfairness that jurisdiction becomes unreasonable.
- Ford Motor removed a strict causal requirement but did not dispense with a strong relationship between the contacts and the claim.

Memory line
Purposeful contact + related claim + fairness.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Civil Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Once a federal court has original jurisdiction over an anchor claim, 28 U.S.C. § 1367 usually permits additional claims sharing the same Article III case or controversy, commonly described as a common nucleus of operative facts. In diversity-only cases, § 1367(b) restricts certain claims by plaintiffs against parties brought in under specified joinder rules when those claims would evade complete-diversity requirements.

Elements / test
- At least one claim must independently fall within original federal jurisdiction.
- The proposed supplemental claim must be sufficiently related to belong to the same constitutional case or controversy.
- The anchor and supplemental claims ordinarily must share a common nucleus of operative facts.
- In a diversity-only case, plaintiffs cannot use supplemental jurisdiction for claims against parties joined under Rules 14, 19, 20, or 24 when doing so would conflict with diversity rules.
- Section 1367(c) allows discretionary refusal for novel or complex state issues, state-law predominance, dismissal of all federal claims, or another exceptional and compelling reason.

Exceptions / limitations
- The diversity limitation is designed to prevent plaintiffs from using joinder to bypass complete diversity.
- A court commonly declines remaining state claims after all federal claims leave the case, although the decision is discretionary.
- Section 1367(d) suspends the limitations period while the supplemental claim is pending and generally for 30 days after dismissal.
- Under Allapattah, related class or multi-plaintiff claims may sometimes proceed despite an individual failure to meet the amount requirement when at least one proper plaintiff meets it.
- Supplemental jurisdiction never stands alone; without a valid anchor claim, § 1367 supplies no jurisdiction.

Memory line
Same case or controversy, then check the diversity plaintiff limits.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Civil Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Federal venue identifies the proper judicial district. Under § 1391, venue ordinarily lies where any defendant resides if all defendants reside in the same state, where a substantial portion of the relevant events or property is located, or, if neither route provides a district, wherever a defendant is subject to personal jurisdiction for the action.

Elements / test
- Residence-based venue is available in a district where any defendant resides when all defendants reside in one state.
- Events-based venue is proper where a substantial part of the acts, omissions, or disputed property is located.
- The fallback provision applies only when no district qualifies under the first two routes and then permits a district having personal jurisdiction over a defendant.
- An individual's venue residence is the person's domicile.
- A corporate defendant resides in any district where it would be subject to personal jurisdiction for the claim; special rules allocate residence within a multi-district state.
- A foreign defendant who does not reside in the United States may be sued in any federal district, without affecting venue as to other defendants.

Exceptions / limitations
- A subject-specific venue statute may displace § 1391, as in some patent, copyright, FELA, or admiralty matters.
- An improper-venue objection is waived unless included in the first Rule 12 motion or first responsive pleading.
- For improper venue, § 1406 permits dismissal or transfer in the interest of justice.
- Even when venue is proper, § 1404 permits transfer for convenience and the interest of justice.
- A valid forum-selection clause is ordinarily given controlling weight under Atlantic Marine except in unusual circumstances.

Memory line
Residence, substantial events, then fallback.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Civil Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Section 1404 permits a convenience transfer from a proper venue to another district where the action could have started or to which all parties consent. Section 1406 addresses improper venue and authorizes dismissal or transfer to a proper district. A normal § 1404 transfer carries the transferor forum's choice-of-law rules, while a § 1406 transfer uses the transferee forum's rules.

Elements / test
- For § 1404, venue is proper in the original court, the destination must be permissible, and the judge balances convenience, witnesses, proof, the plaintiff's choice, local interests, and justice; Van Dusen ordinarily carries the original choice-of-law regime.
- For § 1406, the starting venue is improper, and the court chooses dismissal or a justice-serving transfer; the destination court applies its own choice-of-law rules.
- Section 1407 allows the Judicial Panel on Multidistrict Litigation to centralize related federal actions for coordinated pretrial proceedings.

Exceptions / limitations
- The plaintiff's original forum choice receives weight, though the amount of deference depends on the circumstances.
- A valid forum-selection clause substantially alters the § 1404 balance under Atlantic Marine.
- Moving a case between federal districts in the same state is still a venue transfer.
- Absent consent, the transferee district must have been a place where the action could originally have been filed, including proper venue and personal jurisdiction.
- Transfer is committed to the district court's discretion rather than granted automatically.

Memory line
Federal-to-federal uses transfer; outside the federal system uses forum non conveniens.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Civil Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
In a diversity case, a federal court uses state substantive law and federal procedural law. A valid Federal Rule directly addressing the issue governs under Hanna and the Rules Enabling Act. When no controlling Federal Rule exists, Erie analysis considers outcome effects, federal interests, and the twin goals of avoiding forum shopping and unequal treatment.

Elements / test
- The action rests on diversity or another posture requiring Erie analysis.
- No federal substantive claim independently controls the disputed legal issue.
- If a valid Federal Rule squarely answers the question, apply that rule under Hanna.
- Without a directly applicable Federal Rule, use the York, Byrd, and Hanna considerations.
- The twin aims are discouraging forum selection based on a favorable rule and preventing materially unequal administration of law.
- Ask whether choosing federal rather than state law would meaningfully change the litigation's result.
- Apply the substantive law chosen under the governing choice-of-law methodology.

Exceptions / limitations
- Under Klaxon, a diversity court follows the choice-of-law rules of the state in which the federal court sits.
- State statutes of limitation are ordinarily substantive for Erie purposes.
- Rules assigning the burden of proof are generally treated as substantive.
- The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure and Evidence are ordinarily procedural, but they remain subject to the Rules Enabling Act's validity limits.
- Federal common law remains available in limited enclaves such as interstate disputes, certain federal contracts, and admiralty.

Memory line
Federal procedure; state substance.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Civil Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Rule 56 requires summary judgment when the record shows no genuine dispute over a fact that could affect the outcome and the moving party is entitled to judgment under the law. The judge views supported evidence and reasonable inferences in favor of the nonmovant but does not send a claim to trial when the nonmovant lacks evidence on an essential element.

Elements / test
- A motion may be filed within the schedule set by the court and, under the default rule, no later than 30 days after discovery closes.
- The movant must show the absence of a genuine material dispute and may do so by identifying a lack of proof supporting the opponent's case.
- The nonmovant must respond with specific admissible facts showing a triable dispute rather than relying only on allegations.
- The record may include pleadings used as admissions, discovery materials, affidavits or declarations, stipulations, and formal admissions.
- All reasonable inferences are drawn for the nonmoving party, and conflicting evidence is viewed in that party's favor.
- The sufficiency inquiry parallels the Rule 50 standard identified in Anderson v. Liberty Lobby.

Exceptions / limitations
- The court may resolve only part of a claim or isolate particular issues through partial summary judgment.
- Rule 56(d) permits delay or other relief when the opponent shows that needed discovery is not yet available.
- Judges may not decide witness credibility or weigh competing evidence at summary judgment.
- A court may grant judgment on its own initiative only after giving notice and a fair chance to respond.
- A denial is ordinarily interlocutory and not immediately appealable; a grant disposing of the case generally produces an appealable final judgment.

Memory line
No genuine material dispute means no trial is needed.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Civil Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Rule 19 requires joinder, when feasible, of a person whose absence prevents complete relief, threatens the person's claimed interest, or exposes an existing party to a substantial risk of inconsistent obligations. If the person cannot be joined, the court balances Rule 19(b)'s equity-and-good-conscience factors to decide whether to proceed or dismiss.

Elements / test
- A person is required if the existing parties cannot receive complete relief without that person, the person's interest may be practically impaired, or an existing party risks inconsistent obligations.
- If the person is required and joinder is jurisdictionally and procedurally feasible, the court must order joinder.
- If joinder is impossible, evaluate prejudice, ways to reduce that prejudice, the adequacy of a judgment rendered without the person, and whether the plaintiff has an adequate alternative remedy after dismissal.
- The ultimate Rule 19(b) choice is to continue without the absentee or dismiss because the absentee is indispensable.

Exceptions / limitations
- Joint tortfeasors are ordinarily permissive rather than required parties.
- Sovereign immunity can make a required person unavailable and can strongly favor dismissal under Rule 19(b).
- Failure to join a required party may be raised through the pleadings, later before trial, or by the court on its own.
- The court must account for whether joinder would destroy subject-matter jurisdiction, including complete diversity.
- In some public-rights litigation, the public character of the dispute may permit the action to continue despite effects on absent persons.

Memory line
Required, feasible, then indispensable analysis.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Civil Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Service of process supplies formal notice and supports the court's exercise of authority over the defendant. Rule 4 generally gives the plaintiff 90 days after filing to serve a summons and complaint. Permitted methods vary by defendant, and due process independently requires a method reasonably calculated to inform interested persons of the action.

Elements / test
- Complete service within 90 days unless the court extends the deadline.
- An individual may be served personally, through suitable substitute service at the dwelling on a resident, through an authorized agent, or through a method allowed by relevant state law.
- A corporation may be served through an officer, managing or general agent, or another agent authorized by law.
- A defendant may waive formal service; a proper waiver request extends the answer period and can shift service costs after an unjustified refusal.
- Under Mullane, the chosen notice method must be reasonably designed, in context, to tell interested persons about the proceeding.

Exceptions / limitations
- Good cause requires an extension of the 90-day period, and the court may have discretion to extend even without good cause.
- Service abroad may be governed by the Hague Service Convention or another applicable international procedure.
- Minors and incompetent persons are served through the protective procedures supplied by state law or an appropriate guardian.
- Service by publication is a last resort after a diligent search and only when authorized and constitutionally adequate.
- Insufficient service is waived if omitted from the defendant's first Rule 12 motion or first responsive pleading.
- A curable defect may be corrected by amended papers or a renewed, proper attempt at service.

Memory line
Proper person, proper method, proper time.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Civil Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Claim preclusion prevents the same parties or their privies from litigating the same transactional claim after a valid final merits judgment, including theories that could have been brought in the first action. Issue preclusion prevents relitigation of a particular factual or legal issue actually contested, necessarily resolved, and fairly litigated in an earlier case. These doctrines protect finality and consistent adjudication.

Elements / test
- Claim-preclusion requirements:
- The later action involves the same parties or legally recognized privies.
- The modern approach treats claims arising from the same transaction or connected series of transactions as one claim.
- The earlier judgment must have been valid, final, and on the merits.
- The bar reaches matters actually asserted and matters that should have been asserted in the first action. Issue-preclusion requirements:
- The identical factual or legal issue appears in both proceedings.
- The issue must have been genuinely litigated rather than resolved solely by default.
- Resolution of the issue must have been necessary to the earlier judgment.
- The person against whom estoppel is sought must have had a full and fair opportunity to contest the issue.
- Most jurisdictions no longer demand complete mutuality, so defensive and sometimes offensive nonmutual estoppel may be available.

Exceptions / limitations
- A judgment from a court lacking jurisdiction does not receive ordinary preclusive effect on the merits.
- A major intervening change in controlling law can sometimes defeat issue preclusion.
- Defensive nonmutual issue preclusion is generally accepted when the fairness requirements are met.
- Offensive nonmutual issue preclusion is discretionary and depends on factors such as prior opportunity, ability to join, inconsistent judgments, and procedural differences.
- A federal court must give a state judgment the same preclusive force the rendering state would give it.
- A claimant ordinarily cannot split one transaction into successive lawsuits by reserving theories that could have been joined.

Memory line
Claim preclusion bars the whole claim; issue preclusion bars one decided issue.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Constitutional Law
cards.back:
Core rule
State sovereign immunity generally prevents a private party from suing a state, its agencies, or state officials for retrospective treasury-paid relief without consent. The protection extends beyond the Eleventh Amendment's literal wording and can apply in federal court, state court, and some federal administrative proceedings. A state may clearly waive immunity, and Congress may abrogate it through valid Section 5 enforcement legislation using unmistakable language.

Elements / test
- The defendant is the state, an arm of the state, or an official sued in an official capacity for backward-looking monetary relief payable from the state treasury.
- The immunity can operate in federal court, in a state's own courts under Alden, and in certain proceedings before federal agencies.
- A state can consent to suit only through a clear and unambiguous waiver.
- Congressional abrogation requires unmistakably clear statutory text and a valid exercise of Fourteenth Amendment Section 5 power.
- Ex parte Young allows prospective injunctive relief against a responsible state officer to stop an ongoing violation of federal law.

Exceptions / limitations
- Cities, counties, school boards, and similar local entities ordinarily do not share the state's sovereign immunity.
- Prospective relief against an official for a continuing federal violation is available through Ex parte Young.
- The immunity does not bar suits brought by the United States or by another state.
- States may be subject to bankruptcy-court authority in settings where the constitutional bankruptcy structure permits it.
- Section 5 legislation must be congruent and proportional to a documented constitutional problem under City of Boerne.
- A state can sometimes waive immunity through litigation conduct, including voluntary removal to federal court.

Memory line
State damages suit barred unless waiver, valid abrogation, or an exception.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Constitutional Law
cards.back:
Core rule
The dormant Commerce Clause restricts state and local regulation that discriminates against or places excessive burdens on interstate commerce. A discriminatory law is nearly automatically invalid unless the government proves a legitimate nonprotectionist need and the absence of a workable nondiscriminatory alternative. An evenhanded law is reviewed under Pike by comparing its incidental commerce burden with its local benefits.

Elements / test
- For facial, purposeful, or practical discrimination against out-of-state commerce, begin with near-per-se invalidity and require necessity, a legitimate nonprotectionist objective, and no reasonable neutral alternative.
- For an evenhanded rule, apply Pike: the law advances a legitimate local interest, burdens interstate commerce only incidentally, and does not impose a burden clearly excessive compared with the asserted benefits.
- A state ordinarily may not directly control commercial conduct occurring wholly outside its borders.

Exceptions / limitations
- When the state participates in the market as a buyer or seller rather than regulates it, the market-participant exception can remove dormant Commerce Clause scrutiny.
- Congress can expressly authorize state conduct that otherwise would violate the dormant Commerce Clause.
- Certain subsidies and measures connected to traditional state functions may survive even when they favor residents.
- Genuine, evenhanded health, safety, or quarantine measures commonly survive review.
- Municipal ordinances receive the same dormant Commerce Clause analysis as statewide laws.

Memory line
Discrimination gets the toughest review; incidental burdens get balancing.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Constitutional Law
cards.back:
Core rule
Equal protection bars government from unjustifiably treating similarly situated people differently. The classification determines scrutiny: strict scrutiny applies to race, national origin, many state alienage classifications, and burdens on fundamental rights; intermediate scrutiny applies to sex and nonmarital birth; and all other classifications usually receive rational-basis review. The Fifth Amendment imposes the same basic constraint on the federal government through reverse incorporation.

Elements / test
- Identify governmental differential treatment of persons who are similarly situated for the relevant purpose.
- Choose the standard: strict scrutiny requires narrow tailoring to a compelling interest; intermediate scrutiny requires a substantial relationship to an important interest; rational basis requires a rational connection to a legitimate interest.
- Facial discrimination exists when the classification appears in the text of the law.
- Discriminatory administration exists when a neutral rule is enforced differently based on a classification.
- For a facially neutral law, disparate impact alone is insufficient; Washington v. Davis also requires discriminatory purpose.

Exceptions / limitations
- The federal government is subject to equal-protection principles through the Fifth Amendment Due Process Clause.
- A classification formally receiving rational-basis review may face a more searching form of that review when rooted in bare animus.
- Government affirmative-action classifications are reviewed strictly, and SFFA substantially limited the use of race in higher education.
- Age, disability, and wealth classifications ordinarily receive rational-basis review.
- Some disputes, including claims of unconstitutional partisan gerrymandering as such, are nonjusticiable in federal court under Rucho.

Memory line
Classification determines scrutiny.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Constitutional Law
cards.back:
Core rule
The First Amendment, applied to states through the Fourteenth Amendment, limits governmental restrictions on expression. A content-based rule governing protected speech normally receives strict scrutiny, while a content-neutral rule generally receives intermediate scrutiny. Viewpoint discrimination, prior restraints, and regulation in public forums receive especially demanding review, although several defined categories of speech receive reduced or no protection.

Elements / test
- There must be government action burdening speech or expressive conduct.
- A content-based restriction is presumptively invalid and must be narrowly tailored to a compelling interest.
- A content-neutral restriction ordinarily must materially advance an important interest unrelated to suppressing expression and be appropriately tailored.
- Viewpoint discrimination is almost always unconstitutional.
- A prior restraint carries a heavy presumption of invalidity and demanding procedural safeguards.
- A law can be facially overbroad when it substantially reaches protected expression beyond its legitimate applications.
- A vague speech rule can be invalid because it fails to provide fair notice or invites arbitrary enforcement.

Exceptions / limitations
- Incitement, true threats, fighting words, obscenity, defamation, child pornography, and false commercial speech receive reduced or no protection under their separate tests.
- Commercial speech is ordinarily tested under the Central Hudson framework.
- Expressive conduct is analyzed under the O'Brien test when the regulation targets conduct rather than its message.
- Traditional public forums, designated or limited forums, nonpublic forums, and government speech each use different standards.
- Government employees, public-school students, and prisoners possess speech rights that are modified by their institutional settings.
- Content-neutral time, place, and manner restrictions can be valid in public forums when properly tailored and accompanied by adequate alternatives.

Memory line
Ask what triggered the restriction: message, viewpoint, or neutral conduct.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Constitutional Law
cards.back:
Core rule
In a traditional or designated public forum, government may regulate when, where, and how expression occurs if the rule is content neutral, narrowly tailored to a significant interest, and leaves ample alternative communication channels. A limited or nonpublic forum uses the less demanding requirements of reasonableness and viewpoint neutrality.

Elements / test
- For streets, parks, and sidewalks, require content neutrality, tailoring to a significant governmental interest without demanding the least restrictive means, and ample alternative channels.
- A designated public forum is governed like a traditional public forum for as long as the government keeps it open for public expression.
- In a limited public forum, restrictions must be reasonable in light of the forum's purpose and may not discriminate by viewpoint.
- In a nonpublic forum, the government likewise needs a reasonable and viewpoint-neutral rule.
- A content-based restriction triggers strict scrutiny regardless of the forum label, subject to doctrines governing unprotected speech.

Exceptions / limitations
- A licensing or permit scheme cannot give officials standardless or excessive discretion; criteria must be narrow, objective, and definite.
- An injunction directed at speech receives more demanding review than a generally applicable rule.
- Clinic buffer zones can be framed as time-place-manner rules but require careful tailoring under McCullen.
- Speech on private property does not invoke the First Amendment without sufficient state action.
- When the government itself is speaking, the Free Speech Clause ordinarily does not require viewpoint neutrality.

Memory line
Forum first, then the matching speech test.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Constitutional Law
cards.back:
Core rule
Certain narrowly defined categories may be prohibited or regulated because the law treats their expressive value as outweighed by serious harm. These include incitement, true threats, fighting words, obscenity, defamation, child pornography, and false or misleading commercial speech. Even within an unprotected category, government usually may not discriminate based on viewpoint.

Elements / test
- Incitement under Brandenburg requires advocacy aimed at producing imminent unlawful conduct and a likelihood that the conduct will occur.
- A true-threat prosecution requires at least reckless awareness that the communication would be understood as threatening under Counterman and related cases.
- Fighting words are personally abusive face-to-face words likely to provoke immediate violence, a category now read very narrowly.
- Obscenity under Miller requires a prurient appeal, patently offensive depiction of sexual conduct under community standards, and lack of serious literary, artistic, political, or scientific value.
- Defamation receives reduced protection, with public officials and public figures required to prove actual malice for constitutionally protected fault issues.
- Child pornography involving actual children is unprotected independently of the Miller obscenity test.

Exceptions / limitations
- R.A.V. bars viewpoint discrimination even inside many otherwise unprotected speech categories.
- The Supreme Court has applied the fighting-words category extremely sparingly since Chaplinsky.
- Offensive, hateful, or shocking expression remains protected unless it satisfies a recognized exception.
- Abstract support for illegal conduct is protected unless it meets both parts of Brandenburg's imminence test.
- Courts do not create new categorical speech exceptions simply because the expression is disturbing, as illustrated by Stevens.

Memory line
Use a narrow category test, not an 'offensive speech' shortcut.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Constitutional Law
cards.back:
Core rule
Most constitutional rights regulate government rather than purely private behavior. A constitutional claim against a nominally private actor therefore requires state action, such as performance of a function traditionally and exclusively reserved to government, substantial governmental entanglement or joint participation, or governmental authorization or encouragement of the challenged conduct.

Elements / test
- Conduct directly undertaken by a governmental body or official satisfies the state-action requirement.
- Private conduct may count as state action through an exclusive public function, substantial joint action or symbiotic entanglement, or affirmative state authorization, encouragement, or compulsion.
- In narrow circumstances, judicial enforcement of a discriminatory private arrangement itself supplies state action, as in Shelley v. Kraemer.

Exceptions / limitations
- The Constitution ordinarily does not reach purely private discrimination, although statutes such as Title VII, the ADA, or the Fair Housing Act may prohibit it.
- The public-function category is confined to activities historically and exclusively performed by government; operating a school usually is not enough, while administering an election may be.
- Heavy regulation or the possession of a government license, without more, does not transform a private actor into the state.
- Government financial support by itself usually does not establish state action.
- The Thirteenth Amendment and legislation addressing slavery or its badges can reach private conduct without the usual state-action showing.

Memory line
Constitutional claim against a private actor needs a government connection.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Constitutional Law
cards.back:
Core rule
Substantive due process protects certain fundamental liberties from governmental interference regardless of the procedures used. A burden on a recognized fundamental right receives strict scrutiny, while regulation of a nonfundamental liberty usually receives rational-basis review. The modern historical approach asks whether the asserted right is deeply rooted in national history and tradition and implicit in ordered liberty.

Elements / test
- Define the claimed liberty interest with appropriate specificity.
- If the interest is fundamental, require a narrowly tailored law serving a compelling governmental objective.
- If it is not fundamental, uphold the rule when it is rationally related to a legitimate objective.
- Recognized interests include marriage, procreation, contraception, certain family-living choices, refusing medical care, parental control of childrearing, and interstate travel.
- Glucksberg and Dobbs emphasize deep historical roots and consistency with ordered liberty when considering an unenumerated right.

Exceptions / limitations
- Courts approach new substantive-due-process rights cautiously and generally disfavor expanding the doctrine.
- Ordinary economic and social regulation receives rational-basis review rather than Lochner-era heightened scrutiny.
- Dobbs overruled Roe and Casey, returned abortion regulation largely to the political process, and expressed skepticism toward newly recognized unenumerated rights.
- Lawrence and Obergefell currently protect intimate same-sex relations and same-sex marriage, although their reasoning differs from Dobbs's historical methodology.
- Procedural due process asks whether adequate notice and hearing procedures were supplied and is a separate inquiry.
- When a specific constitutional provision governs the asserted right, courts use that provision rather than substantive due process.

Memory line
Fundamental liberty means strict scrutiny; ordinary liberty means rational basis.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Constitutional Law
cards.back:
Core rule
The Takings Clause forbids government from taking private property for public use without paying just compensation. A taking can occur through direct appropriation or physical occupation, or through regulation that goes so far in limiting use that compensation is required. Public use is interpreted broadly to encompass a public purpose, including qualifying economic-development plans.

Elements / test
- A compelled permanent physical occupation is a per se taking even when the occupied area is small.
- A regulation eliminating all economically beneficial use ordinarily triggers Lucas, while a partial regulatory taking is evaluated through Penn Central's economic impact, investment-backed expectations, and character-of-action factors.
- A permit exaction must have an essential nexus to a legitimate land-use concern and be roughly proportional to the proposed development's effects.
- The public-use requirement generally includes any genuine public purpose under Kelo's deferential approach.
- Just compensation normally equals the property's market value when the taking occurs.

Exceptions / limitations
- Public use does not require that the government itself own or physically use the property.
- A restriction preventing a preexisting nuisance generally does not become a compensable taking even if it eliminates economic use.
- A temporary land-use restriction may or may not constitute a taking depending on duration, severity, and the surrounding circumstances.
- Knick allows a property owner to bring a federal takings claim without first completing a state compensation action, although ordinary ripeness principles still matter.
- Some governmental controls over personal property receive more deferential treatment than comparable controls over real estate.

Memory line
Classify the taking before choosing the test.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Constitutional Law
cards.back:
Core rule
The Tenth Amendment reserves undelegated authority to the states or the people, and states possess general police power over health, safety, welfare, and morals. Federalism also bars Congress from ordering state legislatures or executive officials to administer federal programs, although valid federal regulation, preemption, and properly conditioned spending remain available.

Elements / test
- Powers not granted to the federal government remain with the states or the people.
- States retain general police power over health, safety, welfare, and morality.
- Congress may not direct a state legislature to enact a federal program or command state officers to enforce one.
- Congress may still regulate states through an otherwise valid federal power, preempt conflicting state law, or offer funds subject to valid conditions.
- Education, family law, local governmental structure, and much intrastate commercial regulation are traditionally state-centered subjects.

Exceptions / limitations
- A valid federal statute displaces inconsistent state law through the Supremacy Clause.
- Spending conditions must be clearly stated, connected to the funded program, independently constitutional, and not coercive.
- States remain bound by incorporated Bill of Rights protections and other federal constitutional limits.
- Congress may reach traditional state activities when acting within an enumerated power such as commerce authority.
- Anti-commandeering does not forbid federal preemption or voluntary financial incentives that stay within constitutional limits.

Memory line
States have police power; Congress needs an enumerated power.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Constitutional Law
cards.back:
Core rule
Congress may regulate the routes of interstate commerce, the instrumentalities and people or things moving through commerce, and economic activity that substantially affects interstate commerce. Economic activity can be evaluated in the aggregate, while noncommercial local conduct receives a more demanding connection analysis. The Necessary and Proper Clause can support implementation of valid commerce regulation.

Elements / test
- Channels include interstate highways, waterways, air routes, and communication pathways.
- Instrumentalities and moving persons or things include vehicles, aircraft, trucks, and communications equipment.
- For economic conduct, Congress may aggregate similar activity and needs a rational basis for finding a substantial interstate effect; non-economic conduct requires a more particular connection under Lopez and Morrison.
- The Necessary and Proper Clause supplements Congress's commerce authority by permitting appropriate means of carrying it out.

Exceptions / limitations
- A long causal chain involving noneconomic activity receives closer review under Lopez and Morrison.
- Congress may regulate existing commerce but may not force an inactive person to enter commerce, as reflected in NFIB's treatment of the individual mandate.
- Purely local noneconomic conduct cannot be reached merely by attaching a remote commerce rationale.
- Intrastate economic activity can be regulated when its aggregate effects substantially influence interstate commerce under Wickard and Raich.
- Legislative findings can support the connection but do not conclusively establish constitutional power.
- State regulation of interstate commerce is separately limited by the dormant Commerce Clause.

Memory line
Channels, instrumentalities, and substantial effects.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Constitutional Law
cards.back:
Core rule
The anti-commandeering principle prevents the federal government from ordering state legislatures to enact federal policy or requiring state executive officers to administer federal regulatory programs. Congress may instead regulate through federal officers, validly preempt state law, impose generally applicable rules, or offer noncoercive funding conditions.

Elements / test
- A federal command directing a state legislature to pass or maintain specified legislation is unconstitutional under New York.
- A federal directive requiring state executive officers to administer federal law is unconstitutional under Printz.
- Federal preemption of conflicting state law is constitutionally permissible and differs from commandeering.
- Funding conditions are valid when clearly disclosed, related to the program's federal interest, otherwise constitutional, and not so coercive as to become a financial gun to the head.
- Murphy confirms that anti-commandeering applies broadly, including commands governing state regulatory choices and certain information-related duties.

Exceptions / limitations
- Preemption is not commandeering; Congress may prohibit a state rule even without replacing it with a federal regulatory scheme.
- Congress may encourage state cooperation through spending incentives that satisfy the Dole limits.
- A generally applicable federal law may regulate states in the same manner as private actors under Garcia principles.
- A spending offer becomes unconstitutional when the threatened loss is coercive, as with the Medicaid-expansion remedy in NFIB.
- The doctrine distinguishes a command addressed to state government from a law that regulates state and private conduct on equal terms.

Memory line
Congress may regulate, preempt, or incentivize - but not commandeer.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Contracts
cards.back:
Core rule
First determine whether Article 2 or the common law governs. Article 2 covers sales of movable tangible goods identified to the contract; the common law governs services, real estate, employment, insurance, and intangible rights. For a combined goods-and-services transaction, most courts apply the predominant-purpose test to the deal as a whole, while some focus on the portion that produced the dispute.

Elements / test
- Use UCC Article 2 for a contract whose central subject is the sale of goods.
- Goods are movable, tangible items when identified to the contract; money as payment, securities, land, and intangible rights are excluded.
- Apply common law to services, interests in real estate, employment arrangements, and insurance.
- For a mixed deal, consider the contract's wording, the supplier's business, the relative values of the goods and services, and which component predominates.
- A minority gravamen approach applies the law governing the particular component that caused the lawsuit.

Exceptions / limitations
- An agreement transferring an interest in land is governed by common law because land is not a good.
- Software treatment is divided: standardized packaged software is more often treated as a good, while custom development is more often treated as a service.
- Article 2 applies to crops, timber, or minerals to be severed when the seller is responsible for severance under the applicable UCC rule.
- The Restatement guides common-law contracts, and courts sometimes borrow useful UCC principles by analogy.
- Article 2's merchant-specific provisions apply only when the required party or parties qualify as merchants.

Memory line
Goods use Article 2; services and land use common law.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Contracts
cards.back:
Core rule
An enforceable agreement ordinarily requires objective mutual assent, consideration or a recognized substitute, and the absence of a formation defense. Mutual assent usually appears through offer and acceptance. Common law requires a matching acceptance, while Article 2 tolerates open terms when the parties intended to contract and the court has a workable basis for relief, although quantity normally remains essential.

Elements / test
- Show mutual assent through a valid offer and an effective acceptance.
- Consideration requires a bargained-for act, forbearance, or return promise carrying legal value.
- Possible substitutes include promissory estoppel based on a promise, foreseeable and actual reliance, and a need to enforce to avoid injustice; a prior moral or material benefit works only in narrow circumstances.
- Rule out defenses such as incapacity, an unsatisfied Statute of Frauds, illegality, mutual mistake, misrepresentation, duress, or unconscionability.
- For a goods contract, there must ordinarily be a reasonably certain quantity and enough certainty to supply an appropriate remedy.

Exceptions / limitations
- Article 2 can fill omitted price, place, time, and delivery terms, but generally cannot invent the quantity.
- Negotiations, requests for offers, invitations to bid, and most advertisements do not manifest present assent.
- A promise leaving performance entirely to one party's unrestricted choice is illusory and cannot serve as consideration.
- A requirements or output quantity is sufficiently definite when measured by the party's good-faith needs or production.
- A common-law agreement leaving a material term for future agreement is often unenforceable, while Article 2 is more willing to recognize a bargain despite open terms.

Memory line
Offer + acceptance + consideration, then defenses.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Contracts
cards.back:
Core rule
An offer is an objective communication of present willingness to contract on terms that would allow the recipient's assent to complete the bargain. It must be communicated to the intended offeree and contain enough certainty for the governing law. Once made, it gives the offeree the legal power to accept.

Elements / test
- The communication must objectively show a present commitment rather than a willingness to continue negotiating.
- At common law, identify sufficiently certain parties, subject matter, quantity, price or a way to determine it, and performance timing.
- The offer must reach the person or class entitled to accept it.
- Ask whether a reasonable recipient would understand that assent, without further approval, would create a contract.
- For a sale of goods, quantity ordinarily must be reasonably certain, while Article 2 can supply many other missing terms.

Exceptions / limitations
- A preliminary discussion, ordinary price quotation, or request for bids usually invites an offer rather than makes one.
- Most advertisements are solicitations, but a definite, promissory advertisement identifying quantity and the persons entitled to act can be an offer under cases such as Lefkowitz.
- An auction is presumed to be with reserve, making each bid an offer; in a without-reserve auction, putting the item up generally functions as the offer.
- A joke, social promise, or statement made under circumstances showing no serious contractual intent does not create a power of acceptance.
- A public reward announcement can be a unilateral offer accepted by completing the requested performance with the required knowledge.

Memory line
An offer gives the offeree the power to close the deal.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Contracts
cards.back:
Core rule
Acceptance is the offeree's objective assent communicated in a method invited by the offer or otherwise reasonable. Common-law acceptance must match the offer, while Article 2 may form a contract despite additional or different terms. Unless an exception applies, a properly dispatched acceptance is effective when sent rather than when received.

Elements / test
- The offeree must objectively agree to the offered bargain.
- Only the person or class to whom the offer was directed has the power to accept.
- Acceptance ordinarily must be communicated, although a unilateral offer is accepted through the requested performance.
- Use the required method, or any reasonable method when the offer does not specify one.
- Under the mailbox rule, an authorized, correctly addressed, and properly posted acceptance is effective upon dispatch.
- Silence normally has no effect, but it can operate as acceptance through prior dealings, an understood silence provision, or knowing exercise of ownership over offered goods.

Exceptions / limitations
- The mailbox rule does not govern an option acceptance, a rejection, or an offer that requires receipt; those communications generally become effective when received.
- Beginning invited performance creates protection against revocation of a unilateral offer under the modern Restatement approach, while older formulations speak of substantial performance.
- A bilateral offer is ordinarily accepted by a return promise.
- Under § 2-206, an order requesting prompt shipment may be accepted by a prompt promise to ship or shipment; a nonconforming shipment is acceptance plus breach unless timely identified as an accommodation.
- An attempted acceptance after the power has expired is ordinarily treated as a new offer.

Memory line
Accept in the invited way before the offer ends.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Contracts
cards.back:
Core rule
A common-law modification ordinarily needs fresh consideration because performing an existing contractual duty supplies no new exchange. A fair adjustment prompted by unanticipated circumstances, a third-party promise, or rescission followed by a new contract may avoid that rule. Article 2 requires no new consideration for a modification made in good faith, but writing requirements and no-oral-modification provisions may still apply.

Elements / test
- For a common-law agreement, identify new consideration supporting the changed obligation.
- Recognized workarounds include a fair modification responding to unforeseen difficulties, a promise from a third person concerning a duty owed elsewhere, and mutual rescission followed by a replacement agreement.
- For goods, the modification can be effective without consideration if it was sought and made honestly.
- Test the agreement as modified under the Statute of Frauds; if the new deal falls within the statute, obtain the required writing.
- Article 2 generally enforces a signed no-oral-modification clause, including merchant-form provisions under § 2-209.

Exceptions / limitations
- A modification extracted through duress or a bad-faith threat is not enforceable.
- Conduct and reasonable reliance can sometimes waive a contractual requirement that changes be written.
- Jurisdictions differ in the extent to which they enforce a no-consideration modification based on unforeseen difficulties.
- The parties' course of performance can operate as a modification or waiver in a goods contract even without an express amendment.
- Some states impose additional writing rules when the original agreement was written.

Memory line
Common law needs consideration; UCC needs good faith.
