cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Contracts
cards.back:
Core rule
The Statute of Frauds makes specified agreements unenforceable unless supported by a writing signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought. The principal categories are promises made in consideration of marriage, agreements impossible to perform within one year, transfers of land interests, an executor's personal promise to pay estate debts, sales of goods for at least $500, and promises to answer for another person's debt.

Elements / test
- Apply the writing rule to marriage-related bargains, agreements that by their terms cannot finish within one year, land transfers including many leases longer than a year, an executor's personal undertaking, qualifying goods sales, and suretyship promises subject to the main-purpose exception.
- The memorandum must reasonably identify the parties and subject matter and state the material terms required by the governing category.
- The party being sued must have signed or otherwise authenticated the writing.
- Between merchants, a sufficient written confirmation binds the recipient unless the recipient objects in writing within 10 days.

Exceptions / limitations
- For land, a combination such as payment, possession, and valuable improvements may establish part performance.
- Complete performance by one side removes an agreement from the one-year provision.
- Article 2 exceptions include specially manufactured goods after substantial beginning or commitments, an admission in pleadings or testimony, and enforcement for goods accepted or paid for.
- A suretyship promise serving primarily the promisor's own economic interest may fall outside the writing requirement under the leading-object doctrine.
- Promissory or equitable estoppel can prevent reliance on the statute when refusal to enforce would cause serious injustice.
- Electronic records and signatures can satisfy the writing and signature requirements under E-SIGN and UETA.

Memory line
First identify the category; then writing; then exception.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Contracts
cards.back:
Core rule
The offeree's power to accept ends through revocation, rejection, a counteroffer, expiration, death or incapacity, destruction of the subject, or intervening illegality. After termination, the former offeree cannot revive the original offer by accepting; the response instead can function as a new offer.

Elements / test
- A direct revocation must be communicated before acceptance and normally becomes effective upon receipt.
- Indirect revocation occurs when the offeree learns from a reliable source that the offeror took definite action inconsistent with keeping the offer open.
- A rejection ends the power when received by the offeror.
- At common law, a counteroffer proposing changed terms ordinarily rejects the original offer.
- An offer expires at the stated deadline or, if none is stated, after a reasonable time measured by the circumstances.
- Death or incapacity of either party, destruction of the subject matter, or supervening illegality can terminate the power by operation of law.

Exceptions / limitations
- A consideration-supported option remains irrevocable for its option period.
- A merchant's signed written assurance to keep a goods offer open is irrevocable under § 2-205 for the stated or a reasonable period, but no longer than three months without consideration.
- Once invited performance begins, a unilateral offer becomes temporarily irrevocable under the modern rule.
- Reasonable detrimental reliance that the offeror should have expected can make an offer irrevocable under § 87 principles.
- Death or incapacity ordinarily does not terminate a binding option, which can remain enforceable through the relevant estates or successors.

Memory line
No acceptance after termination - unless the offer was protected.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Contracts
cards.back:
Core rule
A common-law offer must state enough material terms for a court to identify breach and award a remedy, commonly including the parties, subject, quantity, price, and performance time. Article 2 normally requires certainty only as to quantity and can fill many other terms when the parties intended to contract and a reasonable remedy is available.

Elements / test
- At common law, identify the parties and subject, state a quantity, provide a price or objective price method, and specify performance time or allow a reasonable-time implication where permitted.
- For an ordinary Article 2 contract, quantity is the indispensable open-term issue.
- Article 2 can provide a reasonable delivery-time price, the seller's place of business as delivery location, a reasonable time, and payment at delivery when the parties leave those points open.
- A requirements or output contract states quantity through the buyer's good-faith requirements or the seller's good-faith output.

Exceptions / limitations
- A service or employment offer may require additional certainty about work scope and duration.
- At common law, leaving price entirely open can defeat formation when the parties showed they would not be bound without later agreement.
- A court may construe a genuine ambiguity against the party who drafted it under contra proferentem.
- Article 2 uses a more forgiving reasonable-certainty standard than traditional common law.
- Prior dealings and trade usage can clarify language or supply contextual terms.

Memory line
Common law wants essential certainty; the UCC mainly insists on quantity.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Contracts
cards.front: Mirror Image and UCC Battle of the Forms
cards.back:
Core rule
At common law, an acceptance changing the offer is a counteroffer rather than an acceptance. Section 2-207 replaces that mirror-image rule for goods: a definite and timely expression of acceptance forms a contract despite additional or different terms unless it is expressly conditional on assent to those changes. Between merchants, additional terms enter the deal unless the offer limits acceptance, the change is material, or timely objection is made.

Elements / test
- Under common law, any material variation from the offer ordinarily operates as rejection and counteroffer.
- Under § 2-207(1), a definite acceptance can create a contract despite new terms unless the response clearly conditions acceptance on the offeror's assent to them.
- Between merchants, additional terms generally become part of the contract subject to the three statutory objections; when either party is not a merchant, they are merely proposals requiring agreement.
- If the writings do not form a contract but both sides perform, § 2-207(3) recognizes a contract consisting of agreed terms plus UCC gap fillers.
- A term materially alters the bargain when surprise or hardship would result from automatic incorporation.

Exceptions / limitations
- Traditional common law may use a last-shot approach when parties perform after exchanging conflicting forms.
- Many courts treat directly conflicting terms through the knockout rule, removing both and using a statutory gap filler.
- In a transaction involving a nonmerchant, an additional term does not enter without acceptance.
- A response expressly conditional on assent to its new terms does not function as an acceptance under § 2-207(1).
- Course of dealing, performance, and trade usage can inform the parties' terms even in a battle-of-forms case.

Memory line
Common law rejects changes; the UCC separates formation from terms.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Contracts
cards.back:
Core rule
Expectation damages place the injured party as nearly as money can in the position full performance would have produced. The ordinary calculation adds the lost value of the promised performance and recoverable incidental or consequential losses, then subtracts costs and losses avoided. Consequential harm must have been foreseeable at formation and proven with reasonable certainty, and the injured party must take reasonable steps to limit loss.

Elements / test
- Begin with the difference between the promised value and the value actually received.
- Add reasonably caused incidental expenses and qualifying consequential losses.
- Subtract expenditures saved and other losses avoided because performance stopped.
- Consequential damages require foreseeability under Hadley and a reasonably certain amount.
- The claimant must reasonably mitigate and cannot recover loss that proper mitigation would have prevented.
- Reliance damages can reimburse foreseeable expenditures when expected profit or benefit cannot be established reliably.
- Restitution can require return of the value of benefits conferred on the breaching party.

Exceptions / limitations
- Damages that are speculative or cannot be established with adequate certainty are not recoverable.
- Lost profits can be awarded when foreseeable and proven with sufficient reliability; many courts no longer apply an automatic new-business bar.
- Emotional-distress damages are ordinarily unavailable for breach, subject to narrow contracts where such harm is especially foreseeable, such as funeral or wedding-related services.
- Punitive damages are generally unavailable unless the conduct independently supports a tort remedy.
- A liquidated amount is enforceable when reasonable in light of anticipated or actual harm and difficulty of proof, but not when designed as a penalty.
- Attorney's fees follow the American Rule and usually require a contract, statute, or other recognized exception.

Memory line
Give the benefit of the bargain, not punishment.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Contracts
cards.back:
Core rule
The parol evidence rule limits proof of earlier or contemporaneous agreements offered to change a final written contract. A completely integrated writing excludes both inconsistent and consistent additional terms, while a partial integration excludes inconsistent terms but may be supplemented by consistent ones. The doctrine does not prevent evidence offered for a separate permissible purpose such as showing invalidity, a condition to formation, ambiguity, or a later modification.

Elements / test
- There must be a written agreement intended as at least the final expression of some terms.
- Determine whether the writing is integrated and, if so, whether it is complete or only partial.
- A complete integration blocks prior or contemporaneous contradictory terms and consistent additions.
- A partial integration blocks contradictions but allows consistent additional terms.
- A merger clause strongly supports complete integration, although it is not always conclusive.
- The judge decides integration; Article 2 generally favors finding only partial integration unless the writing clearly excludes supplementation.

Exceptions / limitations
- The rule does not exclude evidence of fraud, duress, undue influence, illegality, mistake, a condition precedent to formation, ambiguity, reformation, a later change, or contextual course-of-dealing, trade-usage, and course-of-performance evidence.
- A truly separate collateral agreement supported by distinct consideration may be admitted when it naturally would have been left out of the main writing.
- Under § 2-202, an integrated goods writing may be explained or supplemented, but not contradicted, by course of performance, course of dealing, or trade usage.
- Jurisdictions differ between a strict four-corners method and a contextual approach that reviews surrounding evidence before deciding integration or ambiguity.

Memory line
Integration controls prior terms, not validity challenges or later changes.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Criminal Law & Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Miranda governs questioning by known law-enforcement agents when the suspect is both in custody and being interrogated. Before questioning, police must explain the rights to silence, use of statements, retained counsel, and appointed counsel, and any waiver must be knowing, intelligent, and voluntary. An unambiguous invocation requires police to honor the invoked protection, and Miranda's Fifth Amendment counsel rule is distinct from the Sixth Amendment right that attaches after formal charges.

Elements / test
- Custody exists when, considering all circumstances, a reasonable person would not feel free to terminate the encounter and leave.
- Interrogation includes direct questions and their functional equivalent—police words or actions reasonably likely to elicit an incriminating response.
- Police must give substantially complete Miranda warnings before custodial interrogation.
- A waiver must be knowing, intelligent, and voluntary under the totality of the circumstances.
- An invocation of silence must be clear, after which police must scrupulously honor the decision to stop speaking.
- An invocation of counsel must be clear, and under Edwards police must stop custodial interrogation about all offenses until counsel is provided or another valid exception occurs.
- After a 14-day break in custody under Shatzer, police may seek a new waiver and resume questioning.

Exceptions / limitations
- Questions reasonably necessary to address an immediate public-safety danger fall within the Quarles exception.
- Routine booking questions seeking basic identifying information ordinarily are not interrogation.
- Miranda is unnecessary for noncustodial questioning, including many ordinary traffic stops and voluntary station interviews.
- A suspect speaking to an undercover officer or informant generally is not exposed to the police-dominated coercion Miranda addresses.
- A voluntary but unwarned statement may be used to impeach the defendant's inconsistent trial testimony, though not in the prosecution's case-in-chief.
- Physical evidence derived from a voluntary Miranda violation is generally admissible under Patane.
- A volunteered statement not produced by interrogation is admissible despite the absence of warnings.

Memory line
Custody + interrogation triggers Miranda.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Criminal Law & Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
The Fourth Amendment restricts unreasonable governmental searches and seizures. A search occurs when the government invades a privacy expectation society recognizes as reasonable or physically trespasses on a protected person, house, paper, or effect to obtain information. A warrant ordinarily requires a neutral magistrate, probable cause, and particularity, and only a defendant whose own privacy or property interest was invaded may seek suppression.

Elements / test
- The challenged conduct must be governmental or performed by a private person acting at governmental direction.
- A search can arise through Katz privacy analysis—subjective expectation plus objective reasonableness—or through a Jones-type physical trespass undertaken to gather information.
- A valid warrant comes from a neutral and detached magistrate, rests on a fair probability that evidence or contraband will be found, and particularly describes the place and items.
- Standing is personal: the defendant must show an invasion of the defendant's own protected privacy or property interest.

Exceptions / limitations
- Recognized warrant exceptions include search incident to arrest, exigency, automobiles, plain view, consent, Terry stops and frisks, inventories, and special-needs searches.
- There is ordinarily no protected privacy interest in open fields, abandoned property, garbage left for collection, objects exposed to the public, or many records voluntarily held by third parties; Carpenter limits the latter doctrine for extensive cell-site data.
- An overnight social guest generally has a protected interest in the host's home, while a brief commercial visitor may not.
- The home's curtilage receives Fourth Amendment protection, unlike an open field.
- Border and airport screening regimes operate under reduced privacy expectations and special governmental interests.

Memory line
Government action + protected privacy interest + search or seizure.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Criminal Law & Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Plain view allows an officer to seize an item without a separate warrant when the officer is lawfully positioned, has lawful physical access to the item, and already has probable cause to recognize its incriminating nature. The doctrine authorizes seizure from a lawful vantage point; it does not independently permit entry into a protected location.

Elements / test
- The officer must reach the observation point through a valid warrant, consent, or another lawful basis.
- Without further exploratory examination, the item must appear to be evidence, contraband, or a crime's fruit with probable cause.
- The officer must also possess a lawful route of physical access to the object.
- Horton eliminated any requirement that the discovery be accidental or unexpected.

Exceptions / limitations
- Moving or manipulating an object to reveal identifying information is a separate search requiring its own justification, as in Hicks.
- During a lawful Terry frisk, immediately recognizable contraband may be seized under the plain-feel doctrine.
- Some jurisdictions recognize comparable reasoning for an incriminating odor detected from a lawful location.
- Seeing an item inside a home does not authorize entry; the officer must have a lawful basis to be inside or otherwise reach it.
- Observation from lawful public airspace can sometimes provide a valid plain-view vantage point.

Memory line
Lawful vantage point, lawful access, immediately apparent evidence.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Criminal Law & Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Common-law burglary is a breaking and entering of another person's dwelling at night with the contemporaneous intent to commit a felony inside. It is a specific-intent offense, so the criminal objective must exist when entry occurs. Modern statutes commonly broaden the offense by eliminating breaking, night, dwelling, or felony limitations.

Elements / test
- At common law, breaking can be any slight force used to gain entry, entering occurs when any body part or instrument crosses the threshold, the structure must be another's sleeping dwelling, the event must occur at night, and the defendant must then intend a felony inside.
- Modern statutes often treat unauthorized entry as sufficient, cover many structures, apply at any time of day, and require intent to commit any crime rather than only a felony.

Exceptions / limitations
- Fraud, threat, or entry through a chimney can constitute constructive breaking.
- Valid consent ordinarily defeats breaking, but consent obtained through fraud can amount to constructive breaking.
- An intent formed only after entry does not satisfy common-law burglary, though a statute may define the offense more broadly.
- A person ordinarily cannot burglarize that person's own dwelling under the common-law definition.
- Weapon possession, occupancy, or another aggravating circumstance may increase the offense level under statute.
- Burglary does not merge into the intended offense, allowing separate prosecution and punishment when authorized.

Memory line
The criminal intent must exist when entry occurs.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Criminal Law & Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Larceny is the wrongful taking and slight movement of another person's tangible personal property, without consent, accompanied at the time of taking by an intent to keep it permanently or create an equivalent permanent loss. Because it is a specific-intent offense, an honest claim of right or qualifying mistake can defeat liability.

Elements / test
- The acquisition must be trespassory, including a taking without consent or through fraud that transfers only possession.
- Any movement, however small, satisfies asportation.
- Common law limited the subject to tangible personal property, although statutes now reach more forms of property.
- The property must belong to someone with a superior possessory right when taken.
- The intent to deprive permanently must normally coincide with the taking.
- Under continuing-trespass doctrine, a wrongful taking followed later by permanent-deprivation intent can sometimes satisfy concurrence.

Exceptions / limitations
- Larceny by trick occurs when deception transfers possession but not title; false pretenses instead involves fraudulently obtaining title.
- Common law excluded real property, although modern theft statutes may cover interests or items historically outside larceny.
- A cotenant usually cannot steal jointly possessed property from another cotenant under the traditional superior-possession analysis.
- A genuine plan to borrow and return the item ordinarily defeats permanent-deprivation intent.
- A finder commits larceny only when, at the moment of finding, the finder knows the owner or has a reasonable means of identifying the owner and intends to keep the property.
- Many jurisdictions combine larceny, embezzlement, and false pretenses into a unified statutory theft offense.

Memory line
Wrongful possession plus permanent-deprivation intent.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Criminal Law & Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Embezzlement occurs when a person who initially has lawful possession of another's property intentionally and fraudulently converts it in serious violation of the owner's rights. The key distinction from larceny is lawful entrusted possession at the outset rather than a trespassory initial taking.

Elements / test
- The defendant must act with an intent to defraud or unlawfully deprive the owner.
- Conversion requires unauthorized use or control that seriously interferes with the owner's dominion.
- The property must belong to another, although fraudulent misuse of jointly held property can sometimes qualify.
- The defendant originally received lawful possession through entrustment.
- The relationship commonly has a fiduciary or trust-like character, such as employee, agent, trustee, or bailee.

Exceptions / limitations
- A genuine intent and present ability to restore the property may negate fraudulent-conversion intent in some circumstances.
- A sincere claim of ownership or entitlement is a defense because it defeats fraudulent purpose.
- At common law, an employee with mere custody rather than possession may commit larceny; modern theft codes often eliminate that technical distinction.
- Most modern statutes consolidate embezzlement with other theft forms.
- The property's value commonly determines grading even though it is not part of the basic definition.

Memory line
Lawful possession first; fraudulent conversion later.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Criminal Law & Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Criminal responsibility normally requires a voluntary act and the required guilty state of mind to coincide. Common law groups offenses into specific intent, general intent, malice, and strict liability, while the Model Penal Code uses purpose, knowledge, recklessness, and negligence.

Elements / test
- Specific-intent crimes demand a further purpose or objective and include traditional examples such as larceny, burglary, robbery, assault, forgery, false pretenses, embezzlement, first-degree murder, solicitation, conspiracy, and attempt; general-intent crimes require awareness of the conduct and traditionally include battery, rape, kidnapping, and false imprisonment; malice involves reckless disregard of a high risk in murder or arson; strict-liability offenses dispense with mens rea.
- Under the MPC, purpose means conscious object, knowledge means awareness or practical certainty, recklessness means conscious disregard of a substantial unjustifiable risk, and negligence means failure to perceive such a risk when the actor should have done so.
- Transferred intent moves the intent directed at one victim to an unintended victim when the same type of offense results.

Exceptions / limitations
- Voluntary intoxication or an appropriate mistake of fact can negate a specific intent under common law.
- A general-intent offense is ordinarily defeated only by a reasonable mistake of fact.
- A factual mistake does not excuse a true strict-liability offense.
- The required mens rea must exist when the voluntary act occurs.
- Motive explains why a defendant acted but is not the same as the legally required intent.
- Ignorance or mistake of law is normally no defense unless the offense itself requires knowledge of the law or a narrow reliance exception applies.

Memory line
Mens rea must match the element.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Criminal Law & Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Common-law murder is an unlawful killing of a human being with malice aforethought. Malice may be shown by intent to kill, intent to cause serious bodily injury, extreme recklessness toward human life, or a qualifying felony murder. Statutes commonly divide murder into degrees, often reserving first degree for deliberate premeditated killings and specified felony murders.

Elements / test
- The killing must be unlawful rather than justified or excused.
- The defendant's conduct must be both the actual and proximate cause of death.
- The victim must qualify as a human being under the jurisdiction's homicide law.
- Prove one malice theory: purpose to kill, purpose to inflict grievous harm, depraved-heart recklessness, or a death during an inherently dangerous felony such as burglary, arson, rape, robbery, or kidnapping.
- For statutory first-degree murder, look for willfulness, premeditation, deliberation, or an enumerated aggravating circumstance.

Exceptions / limitations
- A valid justification such as self-defense or defense of others removes the unlawfulness element; deadly defense of property is sharply limited.
- Adequate provocation and heat of passion may mitigate murder to voluntary manslaughter.
- Some jurisdictions permit diminished-capacity evidence to negate deliberation or another heightened mental element.
- Legal insanity can excuse the homicide.
- Felony murder is limited by dangerousness, merger, the duration of the felony, foreseeability, and related doctrines.
- Jurisdictions differ between the agency and proximate-cause approaches when a police officer, victim, or other nonfelon causes the death.

Memory line
Any one of the four malice states can support murder.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Criminal Law & Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Voluntary manslaughter is an intentional or malicious killing reduced from murder because adequate provocation actually produced a heat of passion and the defendant acted before a reasonable person would cool. The MPC's extreme-emotional-disturbance formulation is broader and asks whether the disturbance has a reasonable explanation from the actor's situation.

Elements / test
- The defendant committed an intentional killing or otherwise acted with murder-level malice.
- The provocation must be sufficient to cause an ordinary person to lose normal self-control; traditional examples include serious assault, mutual combat, discovering adultery, or an unlawful arrest, but not mere words under the old rule.
- The defendant must actually have acted while emotionally inflamed by the provocation.
- There must not have been enough time for a reasonable person to cool.
- The provoking event must have caused the homicidal response.
- Under the MPC, the killing may be mitigated when committed under extreme emotional disturbance supported by a reasonable explanation or excuse.

Exceptions / limitations
- Words alone traditionally do not constitute adequate provocation, although some modern jurisdictions are more flexible.
- A sufficient cooling period restores murder liability if the other murder elements are present.
- The adequacy inquiry is largely objective, with only limited consideration of the defendant's personal traits.
- Courts disagree about provocation directed at someone other than the eventual victim.
- An honest but unreasonable belief in the need for deadly self-defense can mitigate murder under an imperfect-self-defense doctrine in some jurisdictions.

Memory line
Murderous intent can be reduced when legally adequate passion or imperfect defense applies.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Criminal Law & Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Involuntary manslaughter is an unintended homicide caused either by criminally negligent conduct posing a substantial unjustifiable death risk or by a qualifying unlawful act under misdemeanor-manslaughter principles. It is less culpable than murder because the conduct does not establish malice aforethought.

Elements / test
- The resulting death was not intended.
- Liability commonly rests on gross criminal negligence or on a death during a malum-in-se misdemeanor or another dangerous unlawful act not qualifying for felony murder.
- The conduct must be both the actual and proximate cause of death.
- The facts must fall short of intent, grievous-harm malice, depraved-heart recklessness, and felony murder.
- The MPC often treats reckless homicide as manslaughter and creates a separate, lower offense for negligent homicide.

Exceptions / limitations
- Ordinary civil negligence is not enough; the departure from reasonable care must be gross or criminal.
- Many jurisdictions limit misdemeanor manslaughter to inherently wrongful or dangerous misdemeanors.
- The death must be a foreseeable consequence within proximate-cause limits.
- The victim's contributory negligence is not a complete defense, although it may affect causal analysis.
- Some codes separate reckless manslaughter from negligent homicide rather than using one common-law category.

Memory line
Gross negligence kills = manslaughter; extreme recklessness may be murder.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Criminal Law & Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Terry permits a brief investigative detention based on specific, articulable facts creating reasonable suspicion of criminal activity. A protective frisk requires an additional reasonable suspicion that the person is armed and dangerous and is limited to locating weapons. Both the detention and search must remain reasonably brief and closely connected to their protective and investigative purposes.

Elements / test
- The stop requires more than a hunch but less than probable cause: reasonable suspicion based on articulable facts.
- The frisk requires a separate reason to suspect that the detainee is armed and presents danger.
- Courts consider the totality of facts and rational inferences known to the officer.
- The personal frisk is ordinarily a pat-down of outer clothing directed only at weapons.
- The officer must use reasonably limited means and duration to confirm or dispel the suspicion.
- Michigan v. Long allows a protective vehicle sweep of areas in the passenger compartment where a weapon could be hidden.

Exceptions / limitations
- An uncorroborated anonymous accusation normally lacks sufficient reliability under Florida v. J.L.
- Unprovoked flight in a high-crime area can contribute to reasonable suspicion under Wardlow.
- Immediately recognizable contraband felt during a valid pat-down may be seized under plain feel.
- A detention that becomes equivalent to an arrest requires probable cause.
- An otherwise valid stop can become unreasonable because of excessive length or scope.
- Squeezing or manipulating an object beyond a weapons check exceeds Terry unless another lawful basis exists.

Memory line
Reasonable suspicion of crime permits the stop; danger permits the frisk.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Criminal Law & Procedure
cards.back:
Core rule
Double jeopardy protects against a second prosecution after acquittal or conviction and against unauthorized multiple punishments for the same offense by the same sovereign. Jeopardy begins when the jury is sworn, when the first witness is sworn in a bench trial, or when the court accepts a guilty plea. Blockburger treats two statutory crimes as different only when each contains an element the other lacks.

Elements / test
- Attachment occurs at jury swearing, the first bench-trial witness, or judicial acceptance of a guilty plea.
- Under Blockburger, offenses are the same when one does not require proof of an element beyond those required by the other.
- The protection applies within one sovereign; state and federal governments, and separate states, may count as different sovereigns.
- After attachment, retrial is barred following an acquittal, ordinarily following conviction, and following a mistrial lacking manifest necessity and defendant consent.
- Ashe's collateral-estoppel component bars relitigation of an ultimate factual issue necessarily decided for the defendant.

Exceptions / limitations
- The separate-sovereigns doctrine permits successive prosecutions by a state and the federal government or by two states for the same conduct.
- Retrial is allowed after a mistrial requested or accepted by the defendant or justified by manifest necessity.
- A defendant who successfully appeals generally may be retried, except after an appellate determination equivalent to an acquittal for insufficient evidence.
- A prosecution for a greater or lesser included offense ordinarily bars the other, but a later-occurring fact such as the victim's death can permit a new homicide prosecution.
- The legislature may clearly authorize cumulative punishments for offenses that otherwise overlap.
- The charging analysis and the ultimate sentencing authorization are distinct; separately charged crimes can support cumulative sentences when the legislature permits them.

Memory line
Attachment + same offense + no exception.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Evidence
cards.back:
Core rule
Hearsay is a declarant's out-of-court assertion offered to prove what the assertion says is true. An assertion may be oral, written, or intentionally communicative conduct. The rule does not apply when the statement is used for another purpose, and otherwise the statement must fit an exclusion or exception to be admitted.

Elements / test
- There must be an oral assertion, written assertion, or nonverbal conduct intended to communicate a fact.
- A person made the assertion outside the present trial or hearing testimony.
- The proponent is using the assertion as proof of the fact asserted.
- If one of those requirements is absent, the evidence is not hearsay.
- Common nontruth uses include legally operative words, notice or effect on a listener, evidence of a person's state of mind, and a prior statement used only to impeach.

Exceptions / limitations
- Rule 801(d) treats qualifying prior statements of a testifying witness and statements of an opposing party as nonhearsay.
- Rule 803 exceptions apply without requiring unavailability and include present sense impressions, excited utterances, then-existing mental or physical conditions, medical-treatment statements, recorded recollections, business and public records, market reports, learned treatises, and specified family or historical records.
- Rule 804 exceptions require an unavailable declarant and include former testimony, dying declarations, statements against interest, personal or family history, and forfeiture by wrongdoing.
- Rule 807 supplies a narrow residual route for exceptionally trustworthy and necessary statements meeting its notice requirements.
- For hearsay within hearsay, every level must independently qualify under an exception or exclusion.

Memory line
Purpose controls: truth or some other reason.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Evidence
cards.back:
Core rule
Rule 801(d) labels two groups of truth-offered out-of-court statements as nonhearsay: specified prior statements by a witness who testifies and can be cross-examined, and qualifying statements attributable to the opposing party. The latter category includes the party's own words and adoptive, authorized, employee, and coconspirator statements.

Elements / test
- A testifying witness's prior inconsistent statement is substantive evidence when it was made under penalty of perjury at a trial, hearing, deposition, or other qualifying proceeding; a qualifying prior consistent statement may rebut fabrication or improper influence and rehabilitate; a prior identification is also covered; the witness must testify and be subject to cross-examination.
- An opposing-party statement may be the party's own statement, a statement the party adopted, a statement by an authorized speaker, an employee or agent statement made during and within the relationship's scope, or a coconspirator statement made during and in furtherance of the conspiracy.

Exceptions / limitations
- Silence counts as adoption only when the circumstances naturally called for a denial by a reasonable person.
- A coconspirator statement needs supporting proof of the conspiracy and qualifying relationship, although the court may consider the statement together with other evidence.
- An opposing-party statement need not be based on personal knowledge and is not excluded merely because it is an opinion.
- For the traditional fabrication rebuttal, the prior consistent statement must precede the alleged motive to lie.
- A witness may be cross-examined about a prior identification even if the witness no longer remembers making it.

Memory line
Witness prior statements have conditions; opposing-party statements are offered against the party.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Evidence
cards.back:
Core rule
A present sense impression is a statement describing or explaining an event or condition while the declarant is perceiving it or immediately afterward. Its reliability rests on near simultaneity rather than excitement, and the declarant's availability is irrelevant.

Elements / test
- The statement must describe or explain the event or condition rather than discuss an unrelated matter.
- It must be made during perception or almost immediately after perception ends.
- Any delay must be very short, usually seconds or only a few minutes.
- No shock, fear, or emotional excitement is required.
- The exception applies whether or not the declarant can testify.
- The declarant must have personally perceived the event or condition being described.

Exceptions / limitations
- A statement made hours later ordinarily is too remote to qualify as immediately afterward.
- An excited utterance permits a longer interval when stress continues but separately requires a startling event and an excited state.
- Only the portion concerning what the declarant perceived receives the exception.
- In a criminal case, a testimonial present sense impression can still be excluded by the Confrontation Clause.
- Independent corroboration is not an element, although supporting evidence can strengthen the foundation.

Memory line
Perceive it, describe it, say it immediately.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Evidence
cards.back:
Core rule
An excited utterance is a statement related to a startling occurrence and made while the declarant remains under the stress that occurrence produced. The continuing excitement is thought to reduce reflective fabrication, and the exception does not depend on unavailability.

Elements / test
- A startling event or condition must have occurred.
- The content must relate to that startling occurrence.
- The declarant must speak while still affected by the event's stress or excitement.
- The excited condition must continue when the words are spoken; the permitted interval depends on the circumstances.
- The declarant may be available or unavailable.
- The declarant must have personally experienced or perceived the event.

Exceptions / limitations
- A longer delay requires stronger evidence that the declarant remained under the event's stress.
- Substantial time for calm reflection or planning weighs strongly against admission.
- An answer to a question can qualify if the declarant was still excited rather than reflective.
- Unlike a present sense impression, this exception tolerates more delay but demands a stress response.
- A statement to police about completed past events may be testimonial and barred despite satisfying Rule 803(2), while statements addressing an ongoing emergency are more likely nontestimonial.

Memory line
Startling event + continuing stress + related statement.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Evidence
cards.front: Statements for Medical Diagnosis or Treatment
cards.back:
Core rule
Rule 803(4) admits statements made for medical diagnosis or treatment when their content is reasonably pertinent to that purpose. Covered subjects include history, symptoms, sensations, onset, and general cause. The statement may be made to a physician or another person involved in obtaining care, and the declarant need not be unavailable.

Elements / test
- The purpose of the communication must be diagnosis or treatment.
- The information conveyed must reasonably assist that medical purpose.
- Permissible content includes medical history, current or past symptoms, sensations, onset, and general causation.
- The recipient may be a doctor, nurse, paramedic, therapist, or another person helping arrange treatment.
- The declarant's availability does not matter.
- A parent, caregiver, or family member can provide treatment-related information about the patient.

Exceptions / limitations
- Identifying who was at fault usually is not treatment-related, although perpetrator identity can be pertinent in child-abuse and similar care contexts.
- A statement assigning legal blame or naming a specific responsible person is excluded unless that detail matters medically.
- A communication to an examiner hired solely to build litigation evidence may fail the treatment-or-diagnosis purpose requirement.
- The patient need not be the speaker; another knowledgeable caregiver may make the qualifying statement.
- In a criminal prosecution, the Confrontation Clause can still apply when the communication's primary purpose was investigation rather than medical care.

Memory line
Medical purpose and medical relevance.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Evidence
cards.back:
Core rule
Impeachment attacks a witness's credibility through recognized methods such as inconsistency, bias, sensory or memory defects, untruthful character, qualifying misconduct, convictions, and contradiction. The permissible use of extrinsic evidence differs by method, and rehabilitation is allowed only after the relevant kind of attack.

Elements / test
- For a prior inconsistent statement under Rule 613, the witness ordinarily must receive a chance to explain or deny it, and extrinsic proof is allowed only when other rules, including the collateral-matter limitation, permit it.
- Bias, interest, or motive to lie may be shown through a wide range of proof, including extrinsic evidence.
- A party may expose problems with the witness's ability to perceive, remember, or communicate, sometimes with expert assistance.
- Rule 608(a) permits reputation or opinion testimony that the witness has an untruthful character.
- On cross-examination, the court may allow inquiry into specific conduct probative of truthfulness, but Rule 608(b) forbids proving that conduct with extrinsic evidence.
- Under Rule 609, dishonesty or false-statement convictions receive special treatment, while other felonies require balancing that is especially protective when the witness is the criminal accused.
- Contradiction uses other evidence to show that a factual assertion by the witness is wrong.

Exceptions / limitations
- After an appropriate attack, rehabilitation may include a qualifying prior consistent statement or evidence supporting truthful character.
- Extrinsic proof cannot be used solely to contradict a witness on a collateral point.
- Rule 610 bars using religious beliefs to attack or support credibility.
- A party normally may not bolster a witness before credibility has been attacked.
- Evidence of truthful character becomes admissible only after an attack on that character.
- A conviction more than 10 years old is admitted only under the demanding Rule 609(b) standard and with advance written notice.

Memory line
Attack credibility with the right method and the right proof.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Evidence
cards.back:
Core rule
Rule 404(a) normally forbids using character to infer that a person acted consistently with that trait on the occasion in dispute. A criminal accused may introduce a pertinent trait and thereby open the door to rebuttal, and certain victim-character rules also apply. In civil litigation, character is generally excluded unless it is itself an element. Rule 405 controls whether proof may use reputation, opinion, or specific acts.

Elements / test
- The baseline rule excludes propensity reasoning from character to conduct on a particular occasion.
- A criminal defendant may offer reputation or opinion evidence of a relevant personal trait; the prosecution may rebut and may offer the same trait concerning the accused as allowed by the rule.
- The accused may offer a pertinent trait of the alleged victim, after which the prosecution may rebut and may offer evidence of the accused's corresponding trait.
- A witness's truthfulness character is governed by Rule 608 and ordinarily proved through reputation or opinion.
- When character is an actual element of a civil claim or defense, proof may use reputation, opinion, and specific instances.
- Under Rule 405, circumstantial character proof is limited to reputation or opinion, while essential-element character permits all three methods.

Exceptions / limitations
- Rule 404(b) allows other-act evidence for a nonpropensity issue such as motive, intent, absence of mistake, identity, or common plan.
- Rules 413 through 415 allow prior similar sexual-assault or child-molestation conduct for propensity in the specifically covered cases.
- Habit under Rule 406 concerns a regular, specific response and can be admitted without using character rules.
- Use Rule 609, rather than Rule 404, to evaluate a conviction offered to impeach credibility.
- A reputation witness must show sufficient familiarity with the relevant community and reputation.
- An opinion witness needs personal knowledge supporting the opinion.

Memory line
Character usually cannot prove conduct - unless a rule opens the door.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Evidence
cards.front: Other Acts for a Nonpropensity Purpose
cards.back:
Core rule
Rule 404(b) excludes prior crimes, wrongs, or acts when the only reasoning is that a person has a bad character and therefore acted the same way again. The evidence can be admitted for a genuine nonpropensity purpose, commonly motive, intent, absence of mistake, identity, or common plan, as well as opportunity, preparation, or knowledge, subject to relevance and Rule 403.

Elements / test
- Identify a prior act; it need not be criminal, charged, or followed by conviction.
- Specify the nonpropensity inference, such as motive, required intent, absence of accident, signature identity, linked plan, opportunity, preparation, or knowledge.
- The earlier conduct must be sufficiently connected or similar to make that particular issue more probable.
- Rule 403 must not find unfair prejudice or another listed danger substantially outweighing the legitimate probative value.
- In a criminal case, the prosecution must provide the notice required by Rule 404(b)'s current provisions.
- On request, the court should instruct the jury to use the evidence only for the permitted purpose.

Exceptions / limitations
- Huddleston requires enough evidence for a reasonable jury to find the prior act occurred, not a separate preponderance finding by the judge as a prerequisite to admission.
- A conviction is unnecessary, and even conduct ending in acquittal may sometimes be admitted for a different issue.
- The necessary degree of similarity depends on purpose: identity usually requires a distinctive signature, while intent may require less.
- The proponent may not disguise a forbidden character-conformity inference with a MIMIC label.
- Conduct intrinsic to the charged event may be admitted as part of the transaction without being treated as an outside act under Rule 404(b), subject to other rules.
- A defendant may offer another person's acts to suggest that person committed the offense, commonly called reverse 404(b), under similar relevance principles.

Memory line
MIMIC is a purpose list, not an automatic admission rule.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Evidence
cards.back:
Core rule
Evidence is relevant when it has any tendency to change the probability of a fact that matters to a claim or defense. Rule 402 starts from admissibility, subject to constitutional, statutory, and evidentiary exclusions. Rule 403 permits exclusion only when the probative value is substantially outweighed by unfair prejudice, confusion, misleading effect, delay, wasted time, or needless repetition.

Elements / test
- Rule 401 requires some tendency to make a consequential fact more or less probable.
- The threshold is deliberately low; even a small logical contribution can establish relevance.
- The affected fact must matter under the substantive issues in the case.
- Rule 402 admits relevant proof unless another legal authority excludes it.
- Rule 403 balances probative value against unfair emotional or propensity prejudice, issue confusion, jury misdirection, delay, wasted time, and cumulative presentation.
- Because the danger must substantially outweigh probative value, Rule 403 is written in favor of admission.

Exceptions / limitations
- Trial judges have broad Rule 403 discretion, and appellate courts ordinarily review for abuse of discretion.
- Circumstantial evidence is not disfavored relative to direct evidence.
- Context or background proof can be relevant even when it does not directly establish an ultimate element.
- Under Rule 104(b), conditionally relevant evidence may be admitted subject to later proof sufficient for a jury finding of the connecting fact.
- Under Rule 104(a), the judge decides most preliminary admissibility questions by a preponderance standard.
- Privilege and categorical exclusion rules can keep evidence out even when it is logically relevant.

Memory line
Any probative tendency passes Rule 401; only substantial counterweight excludes under 403.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Evidence
cards.back:
Core rule
Rule 804 exceptions apply only after the declarant is shown unavailable. Unavailability includes a sustained privilege, refusal despite a court order, claimed lack of memory, death or qualifying physical or mental infirmity, or absence despite reasonable good-faith efforts to obtain attendance. A proponent cannot create the absence through wrongful conduct and then rely on it, except that wrongdoing intended to silence the witness triggers forfeiture.

Elements / test
- Rule 804(a) recognizes privilege, court-defying refusal, lack of memory about the subject, death or infirmity, and absence despite process or reasonable procurement efforts.
- A proponent whose misconduct wrongfully caused the absence generally cannot invoke unavailability for that evidence.
- The Rule 804(b) group includes former testimony, dying declarations, statements against interest, personal or family history, and forfeiture by wrongdoing.

Exceptions / limitations
- A deposition can serve as former testimony or otherwise substitute for live evidence when the applicable requirements are satisfied.
- In a criminal case, satisfying Rule 804 does not by itself satisfy the Confrontation Clause.
- A party who intentionally makes a witness unavailable to prevent testimony forfeits both hearsay and confrontation objections to qualifying statements.
- Death establishes unavailability without additional procurement efforts.
- A valid privilege asserted at trial can make the declarant unavailable.
- When absence is the ground, the proponent must show reasonable, good-faith efforts to secure attendance.

Memory line
Unavailability is the gateway; an 804 exception is still required.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Evidence
cards.back:
Core rule
The Confrontation Clause prohibits the prosecution from using a testimonial out-of-court statement against a criminal accused when the declarant does not testify, unless the declarant is unavailable and the accused previously had a meaningful opportunity to cross-examine. Testimonial status turns largely on whether the statement's primary purpose was to establish past facts for prosecution; nontestimonial statements are governed by evidence law alone.

Elements / test
- Determine whether the statement was testimonial: formal investigative statements about completed events and many forensic reports qualify, while statements made to address an ongoing emergency often do not.
- The prosecution must offer the statement against the accused in a criminal proceeding.
- The declarant must be absent from trial or otherwise unavailable for contemporaneous cross-examination.
- The accused must have lacked a prior adequate chance to cross-examine the declarant.
- When those conditions exist, a hearsay exception alone cannot overcome the constitutional bar.

Exceptions / limitations
- Nontestimonial hearsay does not implicate confrontation, though it must still satisfy ordinary evidence rules.
- A defendant who intentionally causes unavailability to prevent testimony forfeits the confrontation objection under Giles.
- Historically recognized dying declarations may survive Crawford despite their testimonial character.
- Statements primarily made to obtain medical care, including many child statements to caregivers, are often nontestimonial.
- Ordinary business and public records created for administration rather than prosecution are generally nontestimonial, but forensic records prepared for trial can be testimonial.
- Bruton separately limits admission of a nontestifying codefendant's confession in a joint trial and may require severance or an effective redaction.

Memory line
Testimonial + unavailable + no prior cross means exclusion.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Evidence
cards.back:
Core rule
Rule 201 permits judicial notice of an adjudicative fact when reasonable dispute is impossible because the fact is generally known within the court's territory or can be accurately and readily verified from an unquestionably reliable source. The court may act at any stage. A civil jury must accept the fact; a criminal jury may choose whether to accept it.

Elements / test
- The fact must be adjudicative—a fact concerning the parties or events of the particular case.
- It must be either commonly known in the court's geographic area or readily verifiable from a source whose accuracy cannot reasonably be challenged.
- The judge may take notice independently or in response to a party's request.
- A requesting party should supply the information needed to establish the Rule 201 basis.
- A party is entitled, on request, to be heard about both whether notice is proper and exactly what fact is noticed.
- Notice can be taken during trial, before trial, or even on appeal.

Exceptions / limitations
- Legislative facts concerning law or policy are not governed by Rule 201's strict adjudicative-fact framework.
- The court must provide an opportunity to contest judicial notice when a party timely requests one.
- In a civil case, the jury receives the noticed adjudicative fact as conclusive.
- In a criminal case, the jury is told that it may accept or reject the noticed fact.
- A genuinely disputed or verification-dependent fact cannot be judicially noticed.
- Private knowledge, contested factual propositions, and matters requiring interpretation fall outside proper notice.

Memory line
Indisputable fact; mandatory in civil, permissive in criminal.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Real Property
cards.back:
Core rule
Adverse possession transfers title after possession remains actual, open and notorious, exclusive, continuous for the statutory period, and hostile to the record owner's rights. The required period varies by state. Once all requirements are met, the possessor owns the land and the former owner's ejectment claim is cut off.

Elements / test
- Open and notorious possession must be visible or discoverable to a reasonably attentive owner.
- Actual possession means physical use appropriate to the character of the land.
- Exclusive possession cannot be shared with the record owner or the public generally.
- Continuity lasts for the full limitations period without abandonment; seasonal use can qualify when normal for that property.
- Hostility means use without the owner's permission; most states apply an objective test, while some require good-faith or bad-faith intent.
- Successive possessors may tack their periods when connected by privity through a voluntary transfer.
- A possessor acting under color of title may gain constructive possession of the entire described parcel, subject to competing possession rules.

Exceptions / limitations
- Public land ordinarily cannot be acquired through adverse possession.
- The limitations period does not run against a future-interest holder until that interest becomes possessory.
- A qualifying disability existing when possession begins may suspend the period under some state statutes.
- A tenant or licensee cannot hold adversely against the landlord or licensor until the permissive relationship clearly ends or is repudiated.
- Some Torrens or registered-land systems exclude adverse-possession claims.
- A successful claim involving a fence or encroachment can establish a new legal boundary.
- Certain states also require the adverse possessor to pay property taxes.

Memory line
Use the land like an owner, openly and without permission, for long enough.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Real Property
cards.back:
Core rule
A deed transfers a real-property interest and provides whatever title covenants its form contains. A general warranty deed protects against title defects from all periods; a special warranty deed covers only defects attributable to the grantor's ownership; and a quitclaim deed conveys whatever interest the grantor has with no title promises. At closing, the merger doctrine ordinarily replaces inconsistent contract obligations concerning title with the deed.

Elements / test
- A general warranty deed includes present covenants—seisin, right to convey, and against encumbrances—and future covenants—quiet enjoyment, warranty, and further assurances.
- A special or limited warranty deed promises only that the grantor did not create covered title defects while owning the property.
- A quitclaim deed transfers the grantor's existing interest, if any, without title warranties.
- Upon delivery and acceptance, title-related promises in the purchase contract ordinarily merge into the deed.

Exceptions / limitations
- Independent collateral promises, such as certain construction or physical-condition obligations, may survive closing.
- Present covenants are breached, if at all, at conveyance and traditionally do not run to later owners.
- Future covenants can run with the land and may be enforced by a later grantee when possession or title is disturbed.
- The limitations period for a present-covenant claim begins at delivery of the deed.
- Recovery for deed-covenant breach is commonly capped by the purchase price plus appropriate interest.
- A deed must be delivered and accepted to transfer title; recording is unnecessary between the parties but affects priority against later claimants.

Memory line
Execution, delivery, acceptance - then ask what covenants survived closing.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Real Property
cards.back:
Core rule
Recording acts determine priority between an earlier unrecorded interest and a later purchaser. A bona fide purchaser gives value and takes without actual, record, or inquiry notice. Race statutes reward first recording, notice statutes protect a later purchaser who lacked notice at acquisition, and race-notice statutes additionally require that purchaser to record first. The shelter rule extends a protected purchaser's priority to later transferees.

Elements / test
- A BFP must acquire by purchase rather than gift or inheritance, give legally sufficient value, and lack every form of notice.
- In a race jurisdiction first recording controls; in a notice jurisdiction a later purchaser without notice prevails upon purchase; in a race-notice jurisdiction the later purchaser must lack notice and record before the earlier claimant.
- Notice can be actual knowledge, constructive notice from a properly recorded instrument in the chain of title, or inquiry notice arising from suspicious circumstances such as another person's possession.
- A transferee from a BFP generally receives the BFP's protection even if the transferee knew of the earlier interest or gave no value.

Exceptions / limitations
- A wild deed outside the chain of title may fail to provide constructive notice.
- Actual, constructive, or inquiry notice prevents the purchaser from qualifying as a BFP.
- A donee or heir is not a purchaser for value, although the shelter rule may protect one who takes from a BFP.
- The shelter doctrine preserves marketability by allowing the protected owner to transfer good title to someone who would not independently qualify.
- Equitable remedies such as reformation can sometimes affect priority, but ordinarily cannot prejudice a protected BFP.
- Title-search duties depend on the jurisdiction's grantor-grantee or tract-index system.

Memory line
Race = record first; notice = take without notice; race-notice = both.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Real Property
cards.back:
Core rule
The traditional leasehold estates are a tenancy for years with a fixed end, a periodic tenancy that renews until notice, a tenancy at will with no fixed duration, and a tenancy at sufferance created by a holdover. Each category determines how the tenancy arises and how it ends.

Elements / test
- A tenancy for years has definite starting and ending points, expires automatically without notice, and generally must be written when it lasts more than one year.
- A periodic tenancy renews for successive periods and ordinarily requires notice equal to a full period, often capped at six months for a year-to-year tenancy; it may arise expressly, from periodic rent, or from a holdover.
- A tenancy at will has no fixed end and may be terminated by either side, although modern statutes usually require reasonable notice; regular rent can convert it into a periodic tenancy.
- A tenant at sufferance remains after expiration without right, leaving the landlord to choose eviction or acceptance of a new tenancy.

Exceptions / limitations
- Most jurisdictions require a signed writing for a lease exceeding one year.
- Death generally does not end a term or periodic tenancy but traditionally terminates a tenancy at will.
- A landlord may sometimes bind a holdover tenant to a new period resembling the former term, subject to statutory limits.
- A valid self-executing termination clause may end the tenancy upon the specified event.
- Assignment and sublease rights depend on lease language and the type of estate.
- State statutes can replace common-law notice periods with specific minimums.

Memory line
Fixed term, repeating period, mutual will, or wrongful holdover.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Real Property
cards.back:
Core rule
A tenant assigns a lease by transferring the entire remaining term; retaining any reversion usually makes the transfer a sublease. An assignee enters privity of estate with the landlord and is liable for rent and land-related covenants while holding the estate. The original tenant remains bound by privity of contract unless the landlord agrees to a novation. A subtenant normally has no direct privity with the landlord.

Elements / test
- An assignment conveys all of the tenant's remaining leasehold interest.
- The assignee has privity of estate with the landlord and therefore owes rent and covenants that run with the leasehold.
- The original tenant retains contractual liability after assignment unless released through novation.
- A transfer of less than the full remaining term, including retention of a reversion, is a sublease.
- The subtenant's legal relationship is with the original tenant rather than directly with the landlord.
- If the assignee defaults, the landlord may still pursue the original tenant on the lease contract absent a release.

Exceptions / limitations
- A lease may restrict or condition transfers, and many jurisdictions require a landlord to act reasonably when consent is withheld in specified settings.
- After assignment, the original tenant commonly functions as a secondary obligor for rent.
- Each successive assignee has privity of estate only while owning the leasehold.
- Rent, repair, and other covenants touching the land follow the leasehold automatically.
- A purely personal promise does not bind an assignee unless the assignee expressly assumes it.
- A novation ending the original tenant's liability requires the landlord's agreement.

Memory line
Whole remaining term = assignment; retained reversion = sublease.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Real Property
cards.back:
Core rule
Under the modern majority approach, a landlord must take reasonable steps to reduce loss after a tenant abandons before the term ends, usually by attempting to relet. The traditional minority approach allows the landlord to leave the unit vacant and continue collecting rent. The landlord's response can also amount to acceptance of a surrender and termination of the lease.

Elements / test
- The tenant left without justification before the lease ended.
- The landlord may accept surrender and terminate, relet on the tenant's account and seek the shortfall, or in some jurisdictions leave the premises vacant and sue as rent accrues.
- Reasonable mitigation commonly includes advertising, showing the unit, and considering suitable replacement tenants.
- Under the old minority rule, the landlord owes no affirmative mitigation duty.
- Damages generally equal unpaid rent minus rent actually received or reasonably obtainable through mitigation.

Exceptions / limitations
- The landlord need not accept an unsuitable replacement proposing a materially different use or presenting substantially greater risk.
- Mitigation does not require expenditures that exceed the reasonably recoverable loss.
- Modern legislation increasingly applies mitigation to commercial as well as residential leases.
- Accepting a replacement tenant for the landlord's own account can constitute surrender by operation of law.
- Jurisdictions differ on whether the landlord or tenant bears the burden of proving reasonable mitigation.
- Advance notice that the tenant will leave may start the landlord's reasonable mitigation obligation earlier.

Memory line
After abandonment, the landlord must act reasonably to reduce the loss.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Real Property
cards.back:
Core rule
Lease abandonment occurs when a tenant physically leaves before the term expires, intends not to return, and lacks the landlord's consent or another legal justification. It breaches the lease and allows the landlord to accept surrender, relet for the tenant's account, or pursue rent subject to the jurisdiction's mitigation rules. Abandoning an easement similarly requires nonuse plus objective evidence of an intent to relinquish it.

Elements / test
- The tenant actually vacates the premises.
- The circumstances show an intention not to resume possession.
- Part of the agreed lease term remains.
- The landlord did not consent to an early termination.
- The landlord may accept surrender, relet while charging the shortfall, or seek accrued rent under the applicable majority or minority rule.
- For an easement, cessation of use must be accompanied by conduct showing permanent relinquishment; silence or nonuse alone is not enough.

Exceptions / limitations
- A temporary departure coupled with an intent to return is not abandonment.
- The landlord's retaking and reletting for the landlord's own benefit may establish surrender by operation of law.
- A tenant who leaves because the premises became constructively evicted may be justified rather than abandoning.
- Personal belongings left at the property can support an inference that the tenant plans to return, depending on context.
- Abandonment of personal property uses a separate doctrine concerning relinquishment of ownership.
- In some jurisdictions, the landlord's recovery of possession terminates the right to future rent while preserving accrued damages.

Memory line
Vacating plus intent not to return is abandonment; landlord response determines the remedy.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Real Property
cards.back:
Core rule
An easement can end through a written release, merger of dominant and servient ownership, abandonment, prescription, estoppel, expiration, disappearance of the necessity supporting a necessity easement, destruction, or condemnation. Mere nonuse, standing alone, does not terminate the right.

Elements / test
- A release is a signed writing by the easement holder that satisfies the Statute of Frauds and relinquishes the right.
- Merger extinguishes the easement when one owner acquires both estates; later separation generally requires a new grant.
- Abandonment requires nonuse plus objective conduct demonstrating an intent permanently to give up the easement.
- Prescription can terminate the easement when the servient owner obstructs it adversely for the statutory period.
- Estoppel applies when the holder's representation or conduct induces reasonable detrimental reliance by the servient owner.
- An easement ends when its stated duration expires or a valid terminating condition occurs.
- An easement by necessity ends automatically when the necessity disappears.
- Destruction or condemnation of the servient property can eliminate the easement.

Exceptions / limitations
- Long nonuse is insufficient without abandonment intent or another recognized ending event.
- Excessive use ordinarily produces an injunction or damages rather than termination of the entire easement.
- A changed use of the dominant estate does not end the easement if the use stays within its permissible scope.
- An unrecorded termination may not bind a later protected purchaser who lacks notice.
- Prescriptive extinguishment requires elements resembling adverse possession directed against the easement right.
- An appurtenant easement ordinarily runs with the estates, while treatment and transferability of an easement in gross depend on its nature and modern jurisdictional rules.

Memory line
Nonuse alone does not end an easement.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Real Property
cards.back:
Core rule
A professional builder-vendor of a new home impliedly promises that the construction was performed in a workmanlike manner and is reasonably fit for habitation. The warranty principally protects against latent serious defects and is often difficult or impossible to disclaim. Many jurisdictions extend protection to later purchasers for a reasonable period. Residential landlords separately owe tenants an implied warranty of habitability.

Elements / test
- The transaction involves newly constructed residential property sold by the professional builder or developer.
- Construction must meet a workmanlike quality standard.
- The finished home must be reasonably suitable for ordinary residential living.
- The claim ordinarily concerns a hidden defect discovered within a reasonable time.
- Covered problems commonly include major structural defects, serious code violations, and conditions materially impairing habitability.
- Some states allow a subsequent buyer to sue if the defect appears within a reasonable period after original construction.
- Remedies may include reasonable repair cost, reduced value, and foreseeable consequential loss.

Exceptions / limitations
- The builder-vendor warranty normally does not govern an ordinary resale of an older home by a nonbuilder.
- Traditional caveat emptor continues to affect many used-home transactions, subject to disclosure and fraud law.
- A disclaimer is often prohibited or examined under strict standards for clarity and fairness.
- A statute of repose may impose an outside limit such as six or ten years after construction.
- Minor cosmetic imperfections ordinarily do not breach the warranty.
- The builder is not responsible for damage caused by the owner's misuse, neglect, or ordinary wear.
- A tenant invoking the landlord's habitability duty generally must provide notice and a reasonable chance to repair before using specified self-help remedies.

Memory line
Commercial suitability protects the intended use from hidden, serious defects.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Real Property
cards.back:
Core rule
Each cotenant has an equal right to possess the entire parcel. A cotenant occupying alone ordinarily owes no rent to the others unless there is ouster, an agreement, or a fiduciary duty, but must share third-party rent. Cotenants may seek contribution for necessary carrying costs, and a joint tenancy can be severed through acts that destroy a required unity.

Elements / test
- Every cotenant may enter, use, and possess the whole property, subject to the equal rights of the others.
- A sole occupant owes no imputed rent absent exclusion, an agreement to pay, or a special fiduciary relationship.
- Ouster occurs when one cotenant actually or constructively denies another cotenant access or clearly claims exclusive ownership.
- A cotenant collecting rent from an outsider must account for each co-owner's proportional share.
- A cotenant paying more than a fair share of taxes, mortgage charges, or other necessary expenses can ordinarily seek contribution.
- A unilateral improvement generally creates no immediate contribution claim, but value added may be credited during partition.

Exceptions / limitations
- A conveyance of one joint tenant's share severs that share; a mortgage severs in title-theory states but not ordinarily in lien-theory states; leases and judgment liens receive differing treatment across jurisdictions.
- Any cotenant may seek partition in kind or, when physical division is impracticable or unfair, partition by sale.
- Actual rent collected from third parties must be shared, unlike the mere rental value of one cotenant's lawful possession.
- A joint tenant's share passes automatically to surviving joint tenants, while a tenant in common's share passes through probate or intestacy.
- Tenancy by the entirety carries special spousal protections and severance rules.

Memory line
Equal possession, shared income, and no rent without ouster or agreement.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Real Property
