cards.front: Easements by Prior Use and Necessity
cards.back:
Core rule
An easement may be implied from an existing use when formerly unified land is divided and a visible or discoverable, continuous use was reasonably necessary at severance. An easement by necessity also requires prior unity and severance, but arises when the division leaves a parcel without legally adequate access, usually requiring strict necessity.

Elements / test
- Prior-use easement: common ownership, a preexisting quasi-easement benefiting one part, apparent or discoverable use, continuity, reasonable necessity, and existence of those facts when the parcel was severed.
- Necessity easement: prior common ownership, a severance that creates landlocked property, strict or jurisdictionally sufficient necessity at severance, and automatic termination when access necessity ends.

Exceptions / limitations
- Apparent prior use need not be literally visible; discoverable underground pipes or sewers can qualify.
- A necessity easement ends without a release once the necessity disappears.
- An existing alternative route can defeat strict necessity even when that route is inconvenient or expensive.
- A landlocked right of way is the classic necessity claim, although other truly necessary uses can sometimes qualify.
- The scope is limited to what is reasonably necessary rather than every convenient use.
- Some jurisdictions imply an easement more readily in favor of a grantee than reserve one in favor of a grantor.
- A prescriptive easement is different and requires adverse-use elements resembling adverse possession.

Memory line
Prior use looks backward; necessity asks whether severance landlocked the parcel.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Real Property
cards.back:
Core rule
A mortgage secures a debt with an interest in real property. In lien-theory states the borrower retains title and the lender holds a lien; in title-theory states the lender receives formal title subject to repayment. After default, the lender may use a judicial foreclosure or an authorized power-of-sale process, subject to priority, redemption, notice, and deficiency rules.

Elements / test
- The mortgage instrument ordinarily must be written, signed by the mortgagor, describe the property, and identify the secured obligation.
- Recording protects the lender against later purchasers and lienholders under the recording act.
- Priority normally follows first in time, subject to special rules such as purchase-money priority.
- Judicial foreclosure uses a court judgment and supervised sale; nonjudicial foreclosure uses a deed-of-trust or mortgage power of sale and must follow statutory notice procedures.
- Before sale, the mortgagor has an equity of redemption that cannot be waived in the original mortgage transaction.
- Some states grant an additional statutory right to redeem for a limited period after sale.
- When proceeds are insufficient, the lender may seek a deficiency judgment unless an anti-deficiency rule limits personal recovery.

Exceptions / limitations
- A purchase-money mortgage financing the acquisition often receives special priority over earlier claims against the buyer.
- A buyer who assumes the mortgage becomes personally liable; a buyer taking subject to it risks the property but does not ordinarily assume personal liability.
- An enforceable due-on-sale clause permits acceleration after an unauthorized transfer unless federal or state law limits it.
- Anti-deficiency statutes may bar or cap the lender's claim after foreclosure.
- Problems proving ownership of the note or chain of assignments can obstruct foreclosure.
- A bankruptcy filing ordinarily imposes an automatic stay against continued foreclosure.
- Some jurisdictions permit reinstatement by curing the default before the sale.

Memory line
Foreclosure wipes out joined juniors, not seniors.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Real Property
cards.back:
Core rule
Under the shelter rule, anyone who receives title from a protected bona fide purchaser ordinarily inherits that purchaser's priority over an earlier unrecorded interest. The later transferee can be a donee, heir, or purchaser with notice. The doctrine protects marketability, but it cannot be used by the original wrongdoer to cleanse that person's own previously created interest.

Elements / test
- An intermediate owner must have acquired protected BFP status against the earlier unrecorded claim.
- The present claimant must take title through that protected owner.
- The transferee receives the same priority even without giving value or despite knowing of the prior interest.
- The person who originally created or held the unrecorded interest cannot reacquire through a BFP and use shelter to defeat the person's own prior obligation.

Exceptions / limitations
- Shelter does not permit the original grantor of the earlier unrecorded interest to wash that interest away through a later BFP transfer back.
- The doctrine works only if the intermediate transferor truly qualified as a BFP.
- It extends existing protection rather than independently turning the later transferee into a BFP.
- The BFP's own recording is not always a prerequisite to shelter, although recording can affect priority and notice under the applicable act.
- If the supposed BFP had legally effective notice of the earlier recorded interest, neither that purchaser nor later transferees gain shelter protection.
- Fraudulent-transfer law and comparable equitable doctrines can override an otherwise asserted shelter claim.

Memory line
A protected title can be passed along.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Torts
cards.back:
Core rule
A negligence plaintiff must establish a duty of care, breach of the applicable standard, factual and proximate causation, and legally recognized damages. The ordinary benchmark is the conduct of a reasonably prudent person in comparable circumstances. Missing any one element defeats the claim.

Elements / test
- Duty asks whether the defendant owed the plaintiff a legal obligation to follow a stated standard of care.
- Breach is conduct falling below that governing standard.
- Causation requires both factual cause, commonly but-for or substantial-factor cause, and proximate cause based primarily on foreseeable harm.
- The plaintiff must suffer actual injury or property loss; nominal negligence damages are not enough.
- Standards vary for professionals, children, people with physical disabilities, and traditionally common carriers or innkeepers.
- A qualifying safety-statute violation can establish duty and breach when the plaintiff belongs to the protected group and suffers the kind of harm the law sought to prevent.

Exceptions / limitations
- Res ipsa loquitur permits an inference of breach when the event usually implies negligence, the responsible instrument was under the defendant's control, and the plaintiff did not cause the event.
- A person confronting a sudden emergency not of that person's own making is judged as a reasonable person facing that emergency.
- Industry custom is relevant evidence of reasonable care but does not conclusively establish or defeat breach.
- Good Samaritan laws commonly protect unpaid rescuers from ordinary-negligence liability while leaving gross or reckless conduct exposed.
- A rescuer is a foreseeable plaintiff because danger predictably invites rescue.
- Statutory noncompliance may be excused by reasonable inability, emergency, greater danger from compliance, or another recognized justification.

Memory line
Duty, breach, causation, damages.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Torts
cards.back:
Core rule
A person ordinarily has no tort duty to rescue or protect a stranger. A duty can arise from a special relationship, creation of the danger, a voluntary undertaking, a contract, or a statute. Once such a duty exists, the actor must use reasonable care in performing it.

Elements / test
- The default rule imposes no affirmative obligation to assist an unrelated person in danger.
- A duty may come from a protective relationship such as carrier-passenger, innkeeper-guest, on-duty employer-employee, landowner-invitee, parent-child, custodian-dependent, school-student, or spouse; from causing the peril even innocently; from beginning a rescue; from a contract; or from legislation.
- After the duty attaches, performance is measured by reasonable care under the circumstances.
- The threatened harm to the particular plaintiff must still be sufficiently foreseeable.

Exceptions / limitations
- Good Samaritan statutes often immunize a volunteer from ordinary negligence but not gross negligence or recklessness.
- A land possessor often owes no warning duty for an open and obvious condition, although modern comparative principles and foreseeable-distraction rules may qualify that statement.
- Social-host liability for an intoxicated guest is limited and differs substantially by jurisdiction.
- A bystander need not undertake a rescue that would expose the bystander to unreasonable personal danger.
- There is ordinarily no duty to control another person without a qualifying relationship, though custodians and therapists with an identifiable threatened victim can face such duties.
- A person who starts helping and then abandons the effort may be liable if the intervention leaves the victim worse off or induces reliance.
- A statutory duty extends only as far as the statute's protected class, risk, and intended scope.

Memory line
No duty by default; relationship, creation, undertaking, contract, or statute creates one.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Torts
cards.back:
Core rule
Negligence liability requires factual cause and a sufficiently close legal connection between the breach and the injury. Proximate cause ordinarily turns on whether the general type of harm was foreseeable. A foreseeable intervening event leaves liability intact; an extraordinary, unforeseeable superseding event may cut it off.

Elements / test
- The general category of injury must be foreseeable, although its precise sequence or severity need not be.
- Foreseeable intervening events commonly include ordinary medical negligence, rescue efforts, some predictable crimes, and ordinary natural forces.
- An extraordinary criminal act, exceptional natural disaster, or highly abnormal third-party negligence can become a superseding cause when not reasonably foreseeable.
- Under the eggshell rule, an unusual vulnerability affects the amount of harm rather than proximate cause.
- The defendant remains responsible for an unexpectedly severe injury produced by the plaintiff's thin skull or comparable condition.

Exceptions / limitations
- A rescuer's foreseeable response usually remains within the causal chain, and ordinary rescue negligence does not automatically supersede.
- Later negligent medical treatment is generally treated as a foreseeable consequence of the original physical injury.
- A competent person's independent suicide often supersedes, but not when the defendant's tort caused an irresistible impulse or severe mental condition leading to it.
- An intervening animal or natural force normally cuts off liability only when unusually extraordinary.
- A criminal act can remain foreseeable in a setting where the defendant's duty specifically concerned that type of misconduct.
- Some emotional-distress doctrines separately require the plaintiff to have been within a zone of physical danger.

Memory line
Actual cause asks what happened; proximate cause asks whether liability should extend that far.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Torts
cards.front: Plaintiff Fault and Assumption of Risk
cards.back:
Core rule
Plaintiff fault is handled through comparative negligence in most jurisdictions and contributory negligence in a small minority. Pure comparative fault always permits a reduced recovery; modified systems bar recovery once the plaintiff reaches the jurisdiction's 50% or 51% threshold. Assumption of risk requires a knowing and voluntary encounter with a known danger and may be express or implied.

Elements / test
- Pure comparative fault reduces damages by the plaintiff's percentage even when that percentage is greater than the defendant's; modified systems bar a plaintiff who is at least 50% at fault in one version or more than 50% at fault in another.
- Express assumption arises from an agreement to accept the risk, subject to contract and public-policy limits; implied assumption requires actual knowledge and voluntary exposure.
- In a contributory-negligence jurisdiction, any qualifying plaintiff negligence can be a complete bar, subject to doctrines such as last clear chance and special standards for children.

Exceptions / limitations
- Comparative negligence ordinarily does not reduce liability for an intentional tort.
- Workers' compensation and employment statutes often displace traditional assumption-of-risk defenses.
- An exculpatory agreement cannot ordinarily waive intentional misconduct or gross negligence and may be invalid for essential public services.
- Some jurisdictions compare plaintiff negligence in strict-liability actions while others use different defenses.
- Last clear chance and the reduced capacity of children can soften the harshness of contributory negligence.
- The precise comparative-fault system is usually statutory and must be identified for the jurisdiction.

Memory line
Compare fault percentages, then separately test knowing acceptance of risk.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Torts
cards.front: Strict Liability for Animals and Dangerous Activities
cards.back:
Core rule
Strict liability imposes responsibility without requiring negligent conduct for injuries caused by wild animals, known dangerous propensities of domestic animals, or abnormally dangerous activities. Recovery is limited to harm flowing from the particular danger that justifies strict treatment.

Elements / test
- A keeper of a wild animal is ordinarily strictly liable for injuries caused by its dangerous characteristics despite careful precautions.
- For a domestic animal, strict liability traditionally requires knowledge or reason to know of the animal's abnormal dangerous propensity, subject to modern dog-bite statutes.
- An activity is abnormally dangerous when it creates a foreseeable and highly significant physical-harm risk, reasonable care cannot eliminate that risk, and the activity is not a matter of common usage.
- Typical examples include blasting, storing explosives or large hazardous-chemical quantities, keeping wild animals, and handling radioactive material.
- The injury must result from the same risk that makes the animal or activity abnormally dangerous.

Exceptions / limitations
- A trespasser generally cannot invoke strict animal or activity liability unless the injury was intentional or the trespass and danger were sufficiently foreseeable under a special rule.
- Knowing and voluntary assumption of the danger can completely bar recovery in some jurisdictions.
- Comparative negligence may reduce damages under the majority approach, while some jurisdictions treat certain plaintiff fault as a complete defense.
- A dog-bite statute may recognize provocation or another express statutory defense.
- Private necessity can alter or reduce strict liability in limited circumstances.
- Many jurisdictions impose strict liability when livestock physically trespass and damage another's land.
- A common carrier compelled to transport hazardous freight is often exempt from strict liability solely for carrying it.

Memory line
Strict liability covers the characteristic risk, not every accident nearby.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Torts
cards.back:
Core rule
Commercial manufacturers, distributors, and sellers may be strictly liable when a product was defective upon leaving their control, reached the user without a substantial change, and caused injury. A defect may be a manufacturing departure, an unreasonably unsafe design, or inadequate warnings or instructions about a nonobvious foreseeable risk. Negligence, warranty, and misrepresentation theories may also be available.

Elements / test
- The defendant must be a commercial participant in the business of selling or distributing the type of product.
- The defect must have existed when the product left that defendant's control.
- The product must reach the user or consumer without a material alteration that created the danger.
- The defect must be both the factual and proximate cause of the injury.
- A manufacturing defect departs from the intended design; a design defect makes the whole product line unreasonably dangerous under the jurisdiction's test; a warning defect involves a foreseeable nonobvious risk that adequate instructions could have reduced.
- Alternative causes of action include strict products liability, negligent design or manufacture, express warranty, merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, and actionable misrepresentation.

Exceptions / limitations
- A casual or occasional seller outside the commercial distribution business ordinarily is not strictly liable.
- A foreseeable bystander can recover even without purchasing or using the product.
- For many prescription products, the learned-intermediary doctrine permits the manufacturer to warn the prescribing professional rather than the patient directly.
- A substantial post-sale alteration or unforeseeable misuse can defeat causation.
- Knowing assumption of the product risk can bar or reduce recovery depending on the jurisdiction.
- Comparative fault commonly reduces products-liability damages.
- A statute of repose can impose an absolute deadline measured from sale or delivery rather than discovery of injury.
- Some jurisdictions recognize a post-sale obligation to warn when serious newly discovered risks and feasible notice make it reasonable.

Memory line
Defect at sale + unchanged product + causation.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Torts
cards.back:
Core rule
Respondeat superior makes an employer vicariously liable for an employee's tort committed within the scope of employment, even when the employer personally acted carefully. The relationship turns mainly on control over how the work is performed. A principal ordinarily is not vicariously liable for an independent contractor, subject to important exceptions.

Elements / test
- An employee relationship is indicated when the employer controls the manner and means of accomplishing the work.
- An independent contractor ordinarily controls the method while the hiring party specifies the desired result.
- Scope usually requires conduct of the general kind hired to perform, within authorized time and space, and motivated at least partly by service to the employer.
- A minor personal deviation is a detour within scope; a major departure for personal purposes is a frolic outside scope.
- An employee's intentional tort is usually outside scope unless the job involves force, the tort is closely related to assigned duties, or it represents foreseeable friction generated by the work.

Exceptions / limitations
- A hiring party can remain liable for an independent contractor's inherently dangerous work, breach of a nondelegable duty, or negligent selection or supervision.
- Vehicle-owner liability can arise through family-purpose doctrines or statutes imposing limited responsibility for a permissive user.
- A partnership is vicariously responsible for a partner's tort committed in the ordinary course or with partnership authority.
- A joint enterprise can create shared vicarious liability when participants have a common purpose and equal control.
- Parents are ordinarily not vicariously liable merely because of the parent-child relationship, although statutes and negligent-supervision principles can impose liability.
- Dram-shop statutes and some social-host doctrines impose liability for providing alcohol to specified intoxicated persons.

Memory line
Employee + scope of employment = vicarious liability.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Torts
cards.back:
Core rule
Negligent infliction of emotional distress permits recovery only in defined settings. The traditional zone-of-danger route covers a plaintiff threatened with physical harm who fears for personal safety and suffers serious distress, historically with physical symptoms. Modern law may also allow closely related bystanders who contemporaneously perceive an injury and direct victims whose special relationship makes severe emotional harm especially foreseeable.

Elements / test
- Under the zone-of-danger test, the plaintiff was personally exposed to physical danger, feared for personal safety, and, under the traditional version, experienced physical manifestations.
- Under Dillon-style bystander recovery, the plaintiff was near the accident, perceived the injury-producing event as it happened, and had a close family relationship with the direct victim.
- Direct-victim cases arise from a preexisting duty or sensitive relationship, such as medical care, mishandling remains, or a false death notification, where emotional injury is a foreseeable result of breach.
- The distress must be serious rather than ordinary grief, anger, or temporary upset.

Exceptions / limitations
- A minority impact rule still requires some physical contact, however slight.
- Emotional distress caused solely by damage to property is usually not compensable under NIED.
- Most bystander jurisdictions limit qualifying relationships to immediate family, with disagreement about siblings and similar relationships.
- Learning of the accident afterward ordinarily fails the contemporaneous-observation requirement.
- Fear-of-disease claims often require actual exposure and a medically reasonable likelihood of developing the condition.
- Some states continue to require objective physical symptoms of the distress.
- Wrongful-death and loss-of-consortium claims are separate causes of action with their own elements.
- Workers' compensation exclusivity can preempt an employee's NIED suit against the employer.

Memory line
NIED needs a recognized relationship to the danger, victim, or duty.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Torts
cards.back:
Core rule
Negligence per se uses a safety-law violation to establish duty and breach when the claimant belongs to the group the law was designed to protect and suffers the kind of injury the law sought to avoid. The plaintiff must still prove factual cause, proximate cause, and damages. Some jurisdictions treat the violation as only a rebuttable presumption or evidence of negligence.

Elements / test
- Identify a statute, ordinance, or regulation serving a protective or safety purpose.
- The defendant must have violated that legal requirement.
- The plaintiff must fall within the intended protected class.
- The injury must be one of the risks the enactment was designed to prevent.
- The statutory violation must factually and proximately cause the injury.
- Actual damages remain necessary.
- Determine the jurisdiction's effect: conclusive duty and breach, a rebuttable negligence presumption, or evidence for the jury.

Exceptions / limitations
- Recognized excuses include reasonable inability despite diligence, an unexpected emergency, incapacity, lack of reason to know compliance was required, or a greater danger created by compliance.
- Violation of a licensing requirement usually does not itself establish negligence per se because lack of a license is not the causal safety breach.
- The enactment must protect safety rather than merely raise revenue or regulate administration.
- A child defendant may receive the child standard or an excuse based on age, intelligence, and experience.
- Compliance with a statute does not conclusively establish reasonable care when ordinary prudence required more.
- The doctrine does not eliminate the separate need to prove causation and loss.
- Comparative or contributory negligence remains available when otherwise applicable.

Memory line
Protected class + protected harm + statutory violation.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Torts
cards.back:
Core rule
The eggshell-plaintiff rule requires a tortfeasor to compensate the full injury actually caused even when a preexisting physical or psychological vulnerability makes the damage unexpectedly severe. The defendant needs to foresee only the general kind of harm, not its extraordinary degree. The principle applies across negligence, intentional tort, and strict-liability claims.

Elements / test
- The defendant's tortious conduct must be a substantial factual and legal cause of the injury.
- The plaintiff has a preexisting condition or unusual susceptibility.
- The resulting injury is greater than an ordinary person likely would have sustained.
- The general category of harm must be foreseeable, but its unusual extent need not be.
- The rule applies to negligence, intentional torts, and strict liability.
- It includes aggravation of an existing condition, unexpected physical severity, and, in modern law, qualifying psychological vulnerability.

Exceptions / limitations
- The rule affects the extent of damages only after the plaintiff independently proves a tort and causation.
- It does not ordinarily extend liability to a completely different and unforeseeable type of injury.
- The plaintiff still has a duty to take reasonable steps to mitigate further loss.
- Damages may be apportioned when a preexisting condition would have caused some harm even without the defendant's conduct.
- A jurisdiction may reduce recovery for deterioration that the condition inevitably would have produced.
- The rule does not eliminate comparative or contributory fault.
- The defendant's ignorance of the vulnerability is irrelevant.
- Ordinary negligent medical aggravation of the original injury is also generally treated as foreseeable.

Memory line
Take the plaintiff as found.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.

cards.subject: Torts
cards.back:
Core rule
Negligence ordinarily uses an objective reasonably prudent person under similar circumstances. A defendant's low intelligence, mental illness, lack of experience, or voluntary intoxication normally does not lower the standard. The law does adjust for physical disability, children, professional skill, and certain special relationships or activities.

Elements / test
- Ask what an ordinarily careful person would have done in the same circumstances.
- Relevant circumstances include emergencies, unusual hazards, foreseeable risks, and superior abilities actually possessed by the defendant.
- A person with a physical disability is compared with a reasonable person having that disability; a child is compared with similar children unless performing an adult activity; a professional follows professional standards; common carriers and innkeepers traditionally owe heightened care.
- Evidence of customary practice can help establish reasonable conduct but is not controlling.
- A qualifying safety-statute violation may establish negligence per se when the protected-class and protected-harm requirements are met.

Exceptions / limitations
- Mental deficiency, insanity, and below-average intelligence ordinarily do not reduce the objective standard.
- Voluntary intoxication does not provide a more forgiving benchmark; the actor is compared with a sober reasonable person.
- A person with superior knowledge, skill, or intelligence must use those enhanced capabilities.
- A sudden emergency not caused by the defendant is considered as part of the circumstances, whether treated as a separate instruction or simply a factor.
- An unforeseeable sudden medical incapacity, such as an unexpected seizure or heart attack, can excuse conduct.
- An adult beginner in a skilled activity usually receives no learner's discount and must meet the ordinary skilled standard.
- A child operating a motor vehicle or engaging in another dangerous adult activity is held to the adult standard.
- The physical-disability adjustment does not excuse failure to take reasonable precautions appropriate to that disability.

Memory line
Objective ordinary care, with limited status-based adjustments.
cards.sourceNote: Original-language restatement of the corresponding card in the user-supplied study PDF; not an official source quotation.
